1:
"Then came he to Derbe and Lystra: and, behold, a certain disciple was there, named Timotheus, the son of a certain woman, which was a Jewess, and believed; but his father was a Greek." The witnesses note Paul's return to Lystra and the introduction of Timothy. Gill marks Paul's courage: at Lystra he had been stoned (Acts 14:19), "and yet goes thither again; none of these things moved him from the preaching of the Gospel"; such zeal and intrepidity. Matthew Henry notes Paul, a spiritual father, here adopting Timothy and caring for his education; a wise and tender father. The witnesses note Timothy — a disciple of a believing Jewish mother and a Greek father — found at Lystra. The witnesses note the return to Lystra. The witnesses note Timothy introduced.

The witnesses note Paul returning to Lystra (where he was stoned) and finding Timothy, of a Jewish mother and Greek father.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's return to Lystra and the introduction of Timothy. The lesson the expositors draw is the courage of Paul and the providential finding of Timothy, his future fellow-labourer. The witnesses note Paul's courage: he "came to Derbe and Lystra" — and Gill marks that at Lystra he had been stoned and left for dead (Acts 14:19), yet returns; none of these things moving him from preaching the gospel; the zeal and intrepidity of the apostle, undeterred by past suffering. The expositors observe the providential finding of Timothy: "a certain disciple was there, named Timotheus" — at Lystra Paul finds Timothy, a young disciple; the providence supplying the apostle with a fellow-labourer; Matthew Henry notes Paul, a spiritual father, here adopting Timothy and caring for his training. The witnesses note Timothy's parentage: "the son of a certain woman, which was a Jewess, and believed; but his father was a Greek" — Timothy of mixed parentage, a believing Jewish mother (Eunice, 2 Timothy 1:5) and a Greek (Gentile) father; the godly mother and grandmother (Lois) having brought him up in the Scriptures (2 Timothy 3:15). The expositors draw the lesson on godly upbringing: Timothy was the fruit of a godly mother and grandmother, who taught him the Scriptures from a child; the blessing of religious education; the home the nursery of the future minister. The witnesses observe the significance of the mixed parentage: Timothy, of a Jewish mother and Greek father, was fitted to minister to both Jew and Gentile; the providence preparing a man suited to the mixed mission; though his mixed parentage raised the question of circumcision (v.3). The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's courage in returning to Lystra, the providential finding of Timothy, and the blessing of a godly upbringing. The verse thus records Paul's return to Lystra and the finding of Timothy — of a believing Jewish mother and Greek father — teaching the courage of the apostle and the providence supplying him a fellow-labourer reared in godliness.
2:
"Which was well reported of by the brethren that were at Lystra and Iconium." The witnesses note Timothy's good character and reputation. Barnes notes "this is of Timothy" — he "was esteemed highly as a young man of piety and promise"; carefully trained in the knowledge of the Holy Scriptures, and therefore the better qualified for his work (2 Timothy 3:15). Gill notes witness was borne to "his piety, virtue, and good conversation." The witnesses note Timothy well reported of by the believers at Lystra and Iconium — a good testimony, the mark of one fit for the ministry. The witnesses note the good report. The witnesses note Timothy's reputation.

The witnesses note Timothy well reported of by the brethren — a good testimony marking him fit for the ministry.

So the witnesses make the verse Timothy's good reputation. The lesson the expositors draw is the importance of a good testimony as a qualification for the ministry, borne by Timothy. The witnesses note the report: "well reported of by the brethren that were at Lystra and Iconium" — Timothy was highly esteemed by the believers in the surrounding region; Gill notes witness was borne to his piety, virtue, and good conversation; Barnes, that he was esteemed a young man of piety and promise. The expositors observe the value of the good testimony: a good report from the brethren is a qualification for the ministry; "moreover he must have a good report of them which are without" (1 Timothy 3:7); Timothy's good character, attested by those who knew him, marking him as fit for the work; the testimony of the church a credential. The witnesses press the ground of the good report: Timothy's piety, his good conversation, his promise; not mere natural gifts, but a godly character; the report of his holiness and usefulness; the foundation of a true qualification. The expositors draw the lesson on character in ministry: the minister must be of good report, his character attested; gifts without character are insufficient; Timothy's good testimony, joined to his gifts, fitting him for the work; the importance of a holy life and a good name. The witnesses observe the breadth of the testimony: "at Lystra and Iconium" — not only his own city, but the neighbouring one bore witness; the good report widespread, his character known and esteemed beyond his home; the breadth of the testimony confirming its truth. The expositors note the foundation of Timothy's fitness: reared in the Scriptures (v.1), of good report (v.2), gifted for the work (v.3); the combination of godly upbringing, good character, and gifts fitting him for the ministry. The witnesses draw the lesson on the qualifications for ministry: a good report, a godly character, attested by the church; Timothy a pattern of the well-reported minister. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Timothy's good reputation, the importance of a good testimony, and character as a qualification for the ministry. The verse thus records Timothy's good reputation — well reported of by the brethren — teaching the importance of a good testimony as a qualification for the ministry.
3:
"Him would Paul have to go forth with him; and took and circumcised him because of the Jews which were in those quarters: for they knew all that his father was a Greek." The witnesses note Paul's circumcising of Timothy and the principle of expediency. Gill notes Paul, perceiving Timothy had grace and gifts, desired him as a fellow-labourer; and circumcised him "because of the Jews," to remove offence. Barnes notes this as an instance of Paul's choosing young men of piety for the ministry. The witnesses note the famous case: Paul, who refused to circumcise Titus (Galatians 2:3) when the gospel was at stake, here circumcised Timothy — not as necessary to salvation, but for expediency, to remove an obstacle to his usefulness among the Jews. The witnesses note the circumcising of Timothy. The witnesses note the principle of expediency.

The witnesses note Paul circumcising Timothy for expediency — to remove offence among the Jews, not as necessary.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's circumcising of Timothy. The lesson the expositors draw is the principle of expediency — becoming all things to all men — distinguished from the cases where the gospel is at stake. The witnesses note the choice: "him would Paul have to go forth with him" — Paul, perceiving Timothy's grace and gifts (Gill), desired him as a fellow-labourer; Barnes marks this as Paul's choosing young men of piety for the ministry; the apostle taking Timothy as his companion and assistant. The expositors observe the circumcising: "took and circumcised him because of the Jews which were in those quarters" — Paul circumcised Timothy, not as necessary to salvation, but because of the Jews, to remove an obstacle; since Timothy's father was a Greek, the Jews knew he was uncircumcised, and this would hinder his usefulness among them; Paul circumcised him for expediency, that he might have free access to the Jews. The witnesses press the famous distinction from the case of Titus: Paul, who steadfastly refused to circumcise Titus (Galatians 2:3-5) when false brethren demanded it as necessary to salvation, and the gospel of free grace was at stake, here freely circumcises Timothy — because the circumstances differed; with Titus the principle of the gospel was at issue, and Paul would not yield; with Timothy it was a matter of expediency, to remove offence, and Paul freely conformed. The expositors draw the lesson on the principle: Paul became all things to all men, that he might gain some (1 Corinthians 9:20-22) — to the Jews a Jew; conforming in things indifferent for the sake of usefulness; yet standing firm where the truth of the gospel was at stake; the wisdom that distinguishes the indifferent from the essential. The witnesses observe the balance Paul keeps: flexible in things indifferent (circumcising Timothy to remove offence), inflexible where the gospel is concerned (refusing Titus); the wisdom of yielding for expediency and standing firm for truth; the difference between accommodation and compromise. The witnesses draw the application: the believer learns from Paul to become all things to all men in things indifferent, for the gospel's sake, yet to stand firm where the truth is at stake; the wisdom of expediency without compromise. The verse thus records Paul circumcising Timothy for expediency — to remove offence among the Jews — teaching the principle of becoming all things to all men, distinguished from the cases where the gospel is at stake.
4:
"And as they went through the cities, they delivered them the decrees for to keep, that were ordained of the apostles and elders which were at Jerusalem." The witnesses note the delivering of the Jerusalem decrees. Barnes explains "the decrees" (Greek dogmata) as the decisions of the Jerusalem council regarding the four things specified (Acts 15:20, 29) — the word meaning a law or edict, here the decision of the council on the question submitted to it. Gill notes they went through Derbe, Lystra, Iconium, and other cities. The witnesses note Paul and his company delivering to the churches the decrees of the Jerusalem council, for them to keep — the apostolic decision settling the question of the Gentiles and the law. The witnesses note the decrees delivered. The witnesses note the apostolic decision.

The witnesses note the Jerusalem decrees delivered to the churches — the apostolic decision settling the Gentile question.

So the witnesses make the verse the delivering of the Jerusalem decrees. The lesson the expositors draw is the apostolic settling of the Gentile question and the churches' guidance by the decision. The witnesses note the delivering: "as they went through the cities, they delivered them the decrees for to keep" — Paul and his company, traveling through the cities, delivered to the churches the decrees of the Jerusalem council, for them to observe; the apostolic decision carried to the churches. The expositors observe Barnes's note on "the decrees" (dogmata): the decisions of the Jerusalem council regarding the four things specified (Acts 15:20, 29 — abstaining from things offered to idols, blood, things strangled, and fornication); the word meaning a law or edict, here the council's decision on the question of the Gentiles and the law. The witnesses press the significance of the decrees: the Jerusalem council had settled the great question — whether the Gentile converts must be circumcised and keep the law of Moses; the decision freeing the Gentiles from the law's yoke, requiring only the few things for peace and conscience; the apostolic settlement of the controversy. The expositors draw the lesson on the guidance of the churches: the churches were guided by the decision of the council; the apostolic decree directing them in the disputed matter; the order and unity of the church maintained by the common decision; the churches not left to confusion, but guided by the apostolic settlement. The witnesses observe the wisdom of delivering the decrees: the decision, made at Jerusalem, was carried to the churches, that all might know and keep it; the unity of the church preserved by the common observance of the decision; the controversy settled and the churches established (the next verse). The witnesses draw the lesson on the church's order: the believer marks the apostolic decision, the delivering of the decrees, and the guidance of the churches by the common settlement. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the decrees delivered, the apostolic settling of the Gentile question, and the order and unity of the church. The verse thus records the delivering of the Jerusalem decrees — for the churches to keep — teaching the apostolic decision settling the Gentile question and the churches' guidance by the common settlement.
5:
"And so were the churches established in the faith, and increased in number daily." The witnesses note the establishing and growth of the churches. Barnes notes the effect of the wise and conciliatory measure (the decrees) was to confirm the churches in the belief of the gospel and to increase them. Clarke notes the disputes about circumcision had unhinged many, and the decrees came in good time, saving the people from uncertainty. The witnesses note the churches established in the faith — confirmed in the doctrine and grace of faith — and growing in number daily; the fruit of the apostolic settlement and the preaching. The witnesses note the churches established. The witnesses note the daily increase.

The witnesses note the churches established in the faith and growing daily — the fruit of the settlement and preaching.

So the witnesses make the verse the establishing and growth of the churches. The lesson the expositors draw is the confirming and increasing of the churches, the fruit of the apostolic settlement and the preaching. The witnesses note the establishing: "so were the churches established in the faith" — the churches were confirmed, strengthened, established in the faith; Gill notes both in the grace and the doctrine of faith; the believers settled and steadfast. The expositors observe Barnes's note on the cause: the wise and conciliatory measure of the decrees confirmed the churches in the belief of the gospel; the settlement of the controversy establishing them; Clarke adds that the disputes about circumcision had unhinged many, and the decrees came in good time, saving the people from uncertainty; the settled question establishing the unsettled. The witnesses press the double fruit: "established in the faith, and increased in number daily" — the churches both confirmed (in faith) and grown (in number); the inward strengthening and the outward increase; the church both deepened and enlarged. The expositors draw the lesson on the establishing of the church: the church is established in the faith — confirmed in the truth, steadfast in the doctrine; the settling of controversy, the clear teaching, the apostolic order, all establishing the believers; the church grounded and settled. The witnesses observe the daily increase: "increased in number daily" — the church growing daily, new converts added; the gospel prevailing, the church enlarging; the increase the fruit of the preaching and the Spirit's blessing. The expositors note the connection of establishment and growth: the established church grows; the church confirmed in the faith and growing in number; the inward health issuing in the outward increase; the settled, steadfast church fruitful in growth. The witnesses draw the lesson on the church's establishment and growth: the believer marks the churches established in the faith and growing daily, the fruit of the settlement and the preaching. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the establishing of the churches, the daily increase, and the fruit of the apostolic order and the gospel. The verse thus records the churches established in the faith and growing daily — the fruit of the settlement and preaching — teaching the confirming and increasing of the churches.
6:
"Now when they had gone throughout Phrygia and the region of Galatia, and were forbidden of the Holy Ghost to preach the word in Asia," The witnesses note the Spirit forbidding them to preach in Asia. Gill describes Phrygia and the route. The witnesses note the remarkable guidance: the Holy Ghost forbade Paul and his company to preach in Asia (proconsular Asia, with Ephesus) — not because Asia was not to hear the gospel (Paul would later labour there), but because the time was not yet, and God was directing them elsewhere (to Macedonia); the Spirit's sovereign guidance of the mission, closing one door to open another. The witnesses note the Spirit forbidding Asia. The witnesses note the divine guidance.

The witnesses note the Spirit forbidding them to preach in Asia — the sovereign guidance closing one door to open another.

So the witnesses make the verse the Spirit forbidding them to preach in Asia. The lesson the expositors draw is the sovereign guidance of the Spirit, closing one door to direct the mission elsewhere. The witnesses note the route and the restraint: "when they had gone throughout Phrygia and the region of Galatia, and were forbidden of the Holy Ghost to preach the word in Asia" — Paul and his company, traveling through Phrygia and Galatia, were forbidden by the Holy Ghost to preach in Asia (the proconsular province, with Ephesus); the Spirit restraining them from that field. The expositors observe the remarkableness of this guidance: the Spirit forbade them to preach the gospel in Asia — not because Asia was not to hear it (Paul would later labour long at Ephesus, Acts 19), but because the time was not yet, and God was directing them elsewhere; the Spirit's sovereign ordering of where and when the gospel should go. The witnesses press the manner of the guidance: the Holy Ghost forbade them — by some revelation, impulse, or prophetic word; the Spirit directing the mission, not leaving it to the apostles' choice; the divine guidance of the gospel's advance. The expositors draw the lesson on the Spirit's sovereign direction: God orders the times and places of the gospel's coming; the Spirit closing one door to open another; the mission directed by God, not by human plan; "the Lord direct their steps" (cf. Proverbs 16:9). The witnesses observe the providence in the closing of the door: the forbidding of Asia was to direct them to Macedonia (v.9-10); God closing the nearer door to send them to the farther field; the providence guiding the gospel to Europe by forbidding Asia; the closed door the means of the open one. The expositors note the submission of the apostles: Paul and his company submitted to the Spirit's guidance, not preaching where forbidden; the obedience that follows the Spirit's direction, even when it crosses their own plans; the servant led by the Spirit. The witnesses draw the lesson on the Spirit's guidance of the mission: the believer marks the Spirit forbidding Asia, the sovereign direction of the gospel, and the closed door that opens another. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Spirit's guidance, the sovereign ordering of the mission, and the submission to the Spirit's direction. The verse thus records the Spirit forbidding them to preach in Asia — directing the mission elsewhere — teaching the sovereign guidance of the Spirit, closing one door to open another.
7:
"After they were come to Mysia, they assayed to go into Bithynia: but the Spirit suffered them not." The witnesses note the Spirit again restraining them from Bithynia. Gill describes Mysia and Bithynia. The witnesses note the second restraint: Paul and his company, having come to Mysia, attempted to go into Bithynia, but the Spirit did not permit them; the divine guidance again closing a door, redirecting the mission toward Troas and Macedonia; the Spirit's repeated ordering of the way. The witnesses note the Spirit restraining Bithynia. The witnesses note the repeated guidance.

The witnesses note the Spirit again restraining them — from Bithynia — repeatedly ordering the way toward Macedonia.

So the witnesses make the verse the Spirit's second restraint, from Bithynia. The lesson the expositors draw is the repeated, sovereign guidance of the Spirit, narrowing the way toward the appointed field. The witnesses note the attempt and the restraint: "after they were come to Mysia, they assayed to go into Bithynia: but the Spirit suffered them not" — Paul and his company, having reached Mysia, tried to go into Bithynia, but the Spirit did not permit them; the second closing of a door, the mission again redirected. The expositors observe the repetition of the guidance: a second time the Spirit restrained them — first from Asia (v.6), now from Bithynia; the divine guidance repeatedly narrowing the way, closing the doors to the side, leaving only the way to Troas and Macedonia; the Spirit persistently ordering the route. The witnesses press the apostles' submission: they "assayed" to go into Bithynia — they planned and attempted it, by their own judgment; but the Spirit suffered them not, and they submitted; the apostles forming plans, yet yielding to the Spirit's restraint; the servant proposing, the Spirit disposing. The expositors draw the lesson on the Spirit's ordering of the way: God, by repeated restraints, directs his servants to the appointed field; the closing of doors to the side leaving open only the way he intends; the mission guided not by the apostles' plans but by the Spirit's direction; "a man's heart deviseth his way: but the LORD directeth his steps" (Proverbs 16:9). The witnesses observe the providence in the repeated restraint: the closing of Asia and Bithynia was to bring them to Troas, where the Macedonian vision would come (v.9); the doors closed to the side opening the way to Europe; the providence guiding the gospel to Macedonia by these restraints. The expositors note the mystery and the wisdom of the guidance: the apostles did not know why they were restrained, nor where they were being led; yet they submitted, and the purpose was revealed at Troas; the wisdom of following the Spirit's guidance even when its end is hidden. The witnesses draw the lesson on the Spirit's repeated guidance: the believer marks the second restraint, the narrowing of the way, and the submission to the Spirit's direction. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Spirit restraining Bithynia, the repeated ordering of the way, and the providence directing the mission. The verse thus records the Spirit's second restraint, from Bithynia — redirecting the mission — teaching the repeated, sovereign guidance of the Spirit, narrowing the way toward the appointed field.
8:
"And they passing by Mysia came down to Troas." The witnesses note their arrival at Troas. Barnes notes Troas was a city of Phrygia or Mysia on the Hellespont, near the site of ancient Troy; mentioned several times in the New Testament (2 Corinthians 2:12; 2 Timothy 4:13; Acts 20:5). The witnesses note Paul and his company, having been turned aside from Asia and Bithynia, passing through Mysia and coming down to Troas — the seaport on the edge of Asia, the appointed place where the Macedonian vision would come and the gospel turn toward Europe. The witnesses note the arrival at Troas. The witnesses note the place of the vision.

The witnesses note the arrival at Troas — the appointed place where the gospel would turn toward Europe.

So the witnesses make the verse the arrival at Troas. The lesson the expositors draw is the providential bringing of the apostles to Troas, the threshold of the European mission. The witnesses note the journey: "passing by Mysia came down to Troas" — Paul and his company, having been restrained from Asia and Bithynia, passed through Mysia and came down to Troas; Barnes notes Troas was a city on the Hellespont, near the site of ancient Troy, a seaport on the edge of Asia. The expositors observe the providence in the route: the closing of the doors to Asia and Bithynia (v.6-7) had brought them, step by step, to Troas; the seaport at the edge of Asia, facing Europe across the sea; the providence leading them, by the restraints, to the very threshold of the European mission. The witnesses press the significance of Troas: it was at Troas that the Macedonian vision would come (v.9), and from Troas that the gospel would cross to Europe; the place where the great turn would be made; the providence bringing them to the appointed place. The expositors draw the lesson on the providence guiding to the appointed place: God, by the restraints, led the apostles to Troas — the place where his purpose would be revealed; the closed doors bringing them to the open one; the providence directing every step to the appointed end. The witnesses observe the hiddenness of the purpose till Troas: the apostles, restrained from Asia and Bithynia, did not yet know where they were being led; but at Troas the purpose would be revealed (v.9); the providence leading them, the end hidden till the appointed moment. The expositors note the importance of Troas in the New Testament: a place Paul would visit again (2 Corinthians 2:12; Acts 20:5-12); the seaport linking Asia and Europe; the gateway of the gospel to the West. The witnesses draw the lesson on the providence bringing to the appointed place: the believer marks the arrival at Troas, the providence leading by the restraints, and the threshold of the European mission. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the coming to Troas, the providence guiding the steps, and the appointed place of the vision. The verse thus records the arrival at Troas — the threshold of Europe — teaching the providential bringing of the apostles to the appointed place where the gospel would turn toward Europe.
9:
"And a vision appeared to Paul in the night; There stood a man of Macedonia, and prayed him, saying, Come over into Macedonia, and help us." The witnesses note the Macedonian vision. Barnes notes the appearance of a man known to be of Macedonia (probably by his dress and language); whether in a dream or a waking vision is uncertain, God making his will known in both ways; and rejects Grotius's notion of a guardian angel of Macedonia. The witnesses note the vision — a man of Macedonia begging Paul to come over and help — the divine call that would carry the gospel across the sea to Europe; the answer to the restraints, the open door revealed. The witnesses note the Macedonian vision. The witnesses note the call to Europe.

The witnesses note the Macedonian vision — "come over and help us" — the call carrying the gospel to Europe.

So the witnesses make the verse the Macedonian vision. The lesson the expositors draw is the divine call that would carry the gospel to Europe, the open door revealed after the closed ones. The witnesses note the vision: "a vision appeared to Paul in the night; There stood a man of Macedonia... saying, Come over into Macedonia, and help us" — a man of Macedonia appeared to Paul in a vision, begging him to come over and help; Barnes notes he was known to be of Macedonia (by dress and language), the vision in a dream or waking state (God making his will known in both ways). The expositors observe the meaning of the vision: it was the divine call to carry the gospel to Macedonia — to cross the sea from Asia to Europe; the open door revealed after the closing of Asia and Bithynia; the purpose of the restraints now made plain; God directing the gospel to Europe. The witnesses press the plea "Come over... and help us": the man of Macedonia represents the need of Europe — the multitudes in darkness, needing the help of the gospel; the cry of the lost for the light; the call to bring the gospel to those who, though they knew it not, needed it; the help the gospel alone can give. The expositors draw the lesson on the divine call to the mission: the gospel goes where God directs; the vision the call to Europe, the appointed field revealed; the mission not the apostles' choosing but God's directing; the open door shown after the closed ones. The witnesses observe the significance of the turn to Europe: this vision marks the gospel's crossing from Asia to Europe — a momentous step in the gospel's advance; the light coming to the West; the providence directing the gospel to the continent that would become its great seat; the Macedonian call the door of Europe. The expositors note the rejection of Grotius's notion: Barnes rejects the idea that this was the guardian angel of Macedonia; it was a vision, the man of Macedonia representing the call of that land; the simple sense the true one. The witnesses draw the lesson on the call to help the needy: the believer marks the Macedonian vision, the call to carry the gospel to those in need, and the divine direction of the mission. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the vision, the call to Europe, and the open door revealed after the closed ones. The verse thus records the Macedonian vision — come over and help us — teaching the divine call that would carry the gospel to Europe and the open door revealed after the restraints.
10:
"And after he had seen the vision, immediately we endeavoured to go into Macedonia, assuredly gathering that the Lord had called us for to preach the gospel unto them." The witnesses note the prompt obedience and the first "we" passage. Barnes marks this as "the first instance in which Luke refers to himself as being in company with Paul" — the "we" showing that Luke joined Paul and Silas about this time and attended him thenceforth. The witnesses note the apostles, having seen the vision, immediately preparing to go to Macedonia — concluding that the Lord had called them to preach there; the prompt obedience to the divine call, and Luke's joining the company. The witnesses note the prompt obedience. The witnesses note the "we" passage.

The witnesses note the prompt obedience to the vision — and Luke joining the company (the first "we" passage).

So the witnesses make the verse the prompt obedience and Luke's joining. The lesson the expositors draw is the immediate obedience to the divine call and the addition of Luke to the company. The witnesses note the prompt obedience: "after he had seen the vision, immediately we endeavoured to go into Macedonia" — the apostles, having seen the vision, immediately prepared to go; no delay, no hesitation; the prompt obedience to the divine call; the open door entered at once. The expositors observe the conclusion they drew: "assuredly gathering that the Lord had called us for to preach the gospel unto them" — they concluded with assurance that the Lord had called them to preach in Macedonia; the vision interpreted as the divine call; the restraints (v.6-7) and the vision together making the will of God plain. The witnesses press the first "we" passage that Barnes marks: this is the first instance where Luke, the writer, refers to himself as in company with Paul — "we endeavoured"; Luke joined Paul and Silas about this time, at Troas, and attended Paul in his travels thenceforth (the "we" passages); the evangelist himself now an eyewitness companion. The expositors draw the lesson on Luke's testimony: the "we" shows that Luke was present, an eyewitness of the events he records; the historian himself a participant; the account from this point the testimony of one who was there; the credibility of the narrative confirmed. The witnesses observe the readiness of the apostles to follow God's call: having sought to go to Asia and Bithynia and been restrained, they now, at the vision, immediately go to Macedonia; the servants ready to go where God directs, when his will is made plain; the prompt obedience that follows the divine call. The expositors note the assurance of the call: "assuredly gathering" — they were certain of the call; the vision, joined to the restraints, leaving no doubt; the servants acting on the clear will of God; the assurance that follows the plain direction. The witnesses draw the lesson on obedience to the divine call: the believer marks the prompt obedience to the vision, the assurance of the call, and Luke's joining the company. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the immediate obedience, the assurance of God's call, and the eyewitness testimony of Luke. The verse thus records the prompt obedience to the vision and Luke's joining — we endeavoured, assuredly gathering the call — teaching the immediate obedience to the divine call and the addition of the eyewitness Luke to the company.
11:
"Therefore loosing from Troas, we came with a straight course to Samothracia, and the next day to Neapolis;" The witnesses note the voyage from Troas toward Macedonia. Gill notes the loosing from Troas (on the Hellespont) and the course to Samothracia (an island in the Aegean) and Neapolis (the seaport of Philippi). Barnes describes Samothracia, peopled from Samos and Thrace. The witnesses note the prompt voyage — a straight course, the favourable passage — carrying the apostles across the sea toward Philippi; the gospel crossing from Asia to Europe; the providence speeding the way. The witnesses note the voyage to Europe. The witnesses note the straight course.

The witnesses note the prompt voyage from Troas — a straight course carrying the gospel across the sea to Europe.

So the witnesses make the verse the voyage from Troas toward Macedonia. The lesson the expositors draw is the prompt and prosperous passage carrying the gospel from Asia to Europe. The witnesses note the voyage: "loosing from Troas, we came with a straight course to Samothracia, and the next day to Neapolis" — the apostles set sail from Troas, came by a straight (direct, favourable) course to the island of Samothracia, and the next day to Neapolis, the seaport of Philippi; Gill and Barnes describe the places along the route. The expositors observe the promptness of the obedience: having concluded the Lord called them (v.10), they immediately set sail; no delay; the prompt entering of the open door; the obedience that follows the call without hesitation. The witnesses press the "straight course": the voyage was direct and favourable, made in good time (two days); the providence speeding the passage, the wind and sea favourable; the swift crossing a token of God's blessing on the obedient mission; the door not only opened but the way made easy. The expositors draw the lesson on the gospel crossing to Europe: this voyage carried the gospel from Asia to Europe — a momentous crossing; the light coming to the West; the providence speeding the apostles to the continent that would become the great seat of the church; the gospel's advance to Europe. The witnesses observe the contrast with a later, harder voyage (Acts 20:6, the return taking five days): here the course was straight and swift; the providence ordering the passage according to his purpose; the favourable voyage to the appointed field. The expositors note Luke's eyewitness detail: the "we" continues, Luke recording the voyage as a companion; the precise route and timing the marks of an eyewitness account; the historian present. The witnesses draw the lesson on the prosperous mission: the believer marks the prompt voyage, the straight course, and the gospel crossing to Europe. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the obedience that set sail at once, the favourable passage, and the providence carrying the gospel to Europe. The verse thus records the voyage from Troas — a straight course toward Philippi — teaching the prompt and prosperous passage carrying the gospel from Asia to Europe.
12:
"And from thence to Philippi, which is the chief city of that part of Macedonia, and a colony: and we were in that city abiding certain days." The witnesses note the arrival at Philippi. Gill and Barnes note Philippi was named after Philip of Macedon (father of Alexander), famous for the battle between Augustus and Antony against Brutus and Cassius; "the chief city of that part of Macedonia, and a colony" — a Roman colony, with the rights of Roman citizens. The witnesses note Paul and his company coming to Philippi, the chief city of that district and a Roman colony, and abiding there some days — the first city of Europe to hear the gospel. The witnesses note the arrival at Philippi. The witnesses note the Roman colony.

The witnesses note the arrival at Philippi — a chief city and Roman colony, the first European city to hear the gospel.

So the witnesses make the verse the arrival at Philippi. The lesson the expositors draw is the providential choice of Philippi, a chief city and Roman colony, as the first European field. The witnesses note the place: "Philippi, which is the chief city of that part of Macedonia, and a colony" — Philippi, named after Philip of Macedon (Gill and Barnes note), famous for the battle of Augustus and Antony against Brutus and Cassius; the chief city of that district, and a Roman colony — a city with the rights and privileges of Roman citizens, a little Rome in Macedonia. The expositors observe the significance of its being a chief city: the apostles directed their labour to the cities of influence (as Antioch, Ephesus, Corinth); Philippi, a leading city, a strategic field; the gospel planted in a center of influence, whence it might spread. The witnesses press the significance of its being a Roman colony: as a colony, Philippi had Roman citizens and Roman law; this would bear on the later events (Paul's appeal to his Roman citizenship, v.37); the Roman character of the city shaping the narrative. The expositors draw the lesson on Philippi as the first European field: at Philippi the gospel was first preached in Europe; the first European converts (Lydia, the jailer); the church at Philippi a firstfruits of Europe, dear to Paul (the Epistle to the Philippians); the gospel's first planting in the West. The witnesses observe the providence in the choice of Philippi: God directed the apostles to this chief city and colony — a strategic, influential place; the providence choosing the field; the gospel planted where it might prosper and spread. The expositors note the abiding "certain days": the apostles stayed some days, seeking the opportunity to preach; the patient beginning, waiting for the open door (the riverside, v.13); the mission begun with quiet waiting. The witnesses draw the lesson on the providential field: the believer marks the arrival at Philippi, the chief city and colony, and the first European field. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the providential choice of Philippi, the strategic field, and the gospel's first planting in Europe. The verse thus records the arrival at Philippi — a chief city and Roman colony — teaching the providential choice of the first European field, where the gospel would first be preached in the West.
13:
"And on the sabbath we went out of the city by a river side, where prayer was wont to be made; and we sat down, and spake unto the women which resorted thither." The witnesses note the riverside prayer place and the speaking to the women. Chrysostom notes the place was a customary place of prayer, by a river side, outside the city; and on the sabbath, when a multitude would gather; and marks Paul's freedom from pride, sitting and speaking to the women, "a woman, and she of low condition." The witnesses note Paul and his company, finding no synagogue, going to the riverside prayer place on the sabbath, and speaking to the women gathered there — the humble beginning of the European mission, the gospel first preached to a few women. The witnesses note the riverside prayer place. The witnesses note the speaking to women.

The witnesses note the riverside prayer place — Paul speaking to the gathered women, the humble beginning of the European mission.

So the witnesses make the verse the riverside prayer place. The lesson the expositors draw is the humble beginning of the European mission, the gospel first preached to a few women at a riverside. The witnesses note the place: "on the sabbath we went out of the city by a river side, where prayer was wont to be made" — finding no synagogue (Philippi having few Jews), the apostles went on the sabbath to the customary place of prayer, by a river side outside the city; Chrysostom notes the Jews prayed not only in synagogues but in set places, and on the sabbath when a multitude would gather; the riverside the place of prayer. The expositors observe the humble setting: not a synagogue, not a great assembly, but a riverside prayer place, and a gathering of women; the European mission beginning in obscurity; the gospel first preached in the West to a few women at a river. The witnesses press the speaking to the women: "we sat down, and spake unto the women which resorted thither" — the apostles sat down (the posture of teaching) and spoke to the women gathered there; Chrysostom marks Paul's freedom from pride, condescending to teach a few women, one of low condition; the gospel preached to the humble. The expositors draw the lesson on the humble beginnings of great works: the European mission, which would fill the continent, began with a few women at a riverside; the great work from the small beginning; God using the humble means and the obscure setting; the mustard seed of the European church. The witnesses observe the readiness to teach the few: the apostles did not despise the small gathering of women, but sat and taught them; the willingness to preach to the few as to the many; the gospel for the humble; Lydia, one of these women, the first European convert (v.14). The expositors note the providence in the riverside meeting: God brought the apostles to this prayer place, where Lydia was, that the first European convert might be won; the providence ordering the humble beginning; the great harvest from the small field. The witnesses draw the lesson on the humble means: the believer marks the riverside prayer place, the speaking to the women, and the humble beginning of the European mission. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the humble setting, the readiness to teach the few, and the providence beginning the great work in obscurity. The verse thus records the riverside prayer place — Paul speaking to the women — teaching the humble beginning of the European mission, the gospel first preached to a few women at a river.
14:
"And a certain woman named Lydia, a seller of purple, of the city of Thyatira, which worshipped God, heard us: whose heart the Lord opened, that she attended unto the things which were spoken of Paul." The witnesses note Lydia's conversion and the Lord opening her heart. Barnes notes she was "a seller of purple" (a valuable colour, a profitable trade), of Thyatira, a worshipper of God (a proselyte or God-fearer). The witnesses note the famous statement: "whose heart the Lord opened" — the Lord opening Lydia's heart to attend to and receive the gospel; the necessity and the sovereignty of God's grace in conversion, the heart opened by God; effectual grace. The witnesses note Lydia's conversion. The witnesses note the Lord opening her heart.

The witnesses note Lydia's conversion — the Lord opening her heart to receive the gospel, the work of effectual grace.

So the witnesses make the verse Lydia's conversion and the Lord opening her heart. The lesson the expositors draw is the necessity and sovereignty of God's grace in conversion, shown in the Lord opening Lydia's heart. The witnesses note who Lydia was: "a seller of purple, of the city of Thyatira, which worshipped God" — Barnes notes she was a dealer in purple (a valuable, profitable trade), from Thyatira (a city famed for dyeing), a worshipper of God (a proselyte or God-fearer, not yet a Christian); a devout woman, attending the place of prayer. The expositors observe the great statement: "whose heart the Lord opened, that she attended unto the things which were spoken of Paul" — the Lord opened Lydia's heart; not Paul's eloquence, nor her own resolve, but the Lord, by his Spirit, opened her heart to attend to and receive the gospel; the work of God in her conversion. The witnesses press the necessity of God opening the heart: the natural heart is closed to the gospel — shut by sin, blindness, unbelief; and it must be opened by God before it will receive the truth; "the natural man receiveth not the things of the Spirit of God" (1 Corinthians 2:14); Lydia heard, but it was the Lord opening her heart that made her attend; the necessity of effectual grace. The expositors draw the lesson on the sovereignty of grace in conversion: it is the Lord who opens the heart; conversion the work of God, not of man; the heart opened by the Spirit, the gospel received by grace; "no man can come to me, except the Father which hath sent me draw him" (John 6:44); the sovereign, effectual grace of God. The witnesses observe the means joined to the grace: Lydia "heard us" — the gospel preached; and the Lord opened her heart through the hearing; the word the means, the Spirit the power; God opening the heart through the preached word; the means and the grace together. The expositors note the evidence of the opened heart: "that she attended unto the things which were spoken" — the opened heart attends, receives, believes; the fruit of the opening, the attending to and embracing of the gospel; the opened heart the believing heart. The witnesses draw the lesson on effectual grace: the believer marks the Lord opening Lydia's heart, the necessity and sovereignty of grace, and the heart opened to receive the gospel. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Lydia's conversion, the Lord opening the heart, and the effectual grace that alone makes the gospel received. The verse thus records Lydia's conversion — the Lord opening her heart — teaching the necessity and sovereignty of God's grace in conversion, the heart opened by God to receive the gospel.
15:
"And when she was baptized, and her household, she besought us, saying, If ye have judged me to be faithful to the Lord, come into my house, and abide there. And she constrained us." The witnesses note Lydia's baptism with her household and her hospitality. Gill notes she was baptized in water, by the river, on her profession of faith. Barnes notes she was baptized "apparently without any delay," with her household; and weighs whether this affords a presumptive proof of household (or infant) baptism. The witnesses note Lydia and her household baptized, and her earnest, constraining hospitality to the apostles — the fruit of faith in baptism and in love; the household baptism debated. The witnesses note the household baptism. The witnesses note Lydia's hospitality.

The witnesses note Lydia and her household baptized — and her constraining hospitality, the fruit of faith.

So the witnesses make the verse Lydia's baptism and hospitality. The lesson the expositors draw is the fruit of faith in baptism and in love, and the debated question of household baptism. The witnesses note the baptism: "when she was baptized, and her household" — Lydia, on her profession of faith, was baptized (Gill notes, in water, by the river), and her household with her; the believer baptized upon faith, and her house also. The expositors weigh the question of household baptism that Barnes raises: Lydia's household was baptized with her, no mention being made of their believing; some take this as a presumptive proof of household baptism, including infants (the paedobaptist reading — the household, children included, baptized on the faith of the head); others hold the household were believers, capable of faith, baptized on their own profession (the credobaptist reading — the household old enough to believe); the case cited on both sides, the Scripture not deciding expressly. The witnesses note the honest difference: the verse records the household baptized, but does not state whether infants were included or all believed; the paedobaptists pressing the household principle, the credobaptists the absence of stated infants; the debate of long standing, the text not conclusive either way. The expositors draw the lesson, holding the substance: whatever the mode and the subjects, the household was baptized — the family embraced in the ordinance; the gospel reaching the house through the head; the household blessed in Lydia's faith. The witnesses press the hospitality: "If ye have judged me to be faithful to the Lord, come into my house, and abide there. And she constrained us" — Lydia, the first European convert, immediately showed the fruit of faith in love — earnest, even constraining, hospitality to the apostles; the new convert opening her home to the servants of Christ; the love that follows faith. The expositors observe the marks of true conversion in Lydia: faith (her heart opened), baptism (her confession), and love (her hospitality); the genuine convert showing the fruit of faith in good works; the opened heart issuing in the open home. The witnesses draw the lesson on faith and its fruit: the believer marks Lydia's baptism, the household embraced, and the constraining hospitality that is the fruit of faith. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the household baptism (debated), the fruit of faith in love, and Lydia's open home. The verse thus records Lydia's baptism and hospitality — she and her household baptized, her constraining love — teaching the fruit of faith in baptism and hospitality, amid the debated question of household baptism.
16:
"And it came to pass, as we went to prayer, a certain damsel possessed with a spirit of divination met us, which brought her masters much gain by soothsaying:" The witnesses note the slave girl with a spirit of divination. Gill notes "going to prayer" means going to the place of prayer (the oratory); the damsel met them as they went, and followed them many days. The witnesses note a slave girl, possessed with a spirit of divination (a Python spirit), who by soothsaying (fortune-telling) brought her masters much gain — the real demonic possession, exploited for profit; the contrast of the gospel with the powers of darkness and greed. The witnesses note the spirit of divination. The witnesses note the gain by soothsaying.

The witnesses note the slave girl with a spirit of divination — possessed and exploited for her masters' gain.

So the witnesses make the verse the slave girl with a spirit of divination. The lesson the expositors draw is the reality of the demonic possession and the exploitation of it for gain. The witnesses note the encounter: "as we went to prayer, a certain damsel possessed with a spirit of divination met us" — as the apostles went to the place of prayer (Gill notes the oratory), a slave girl, possessed with a spirit of divination, met them and followed them (v.17-18 show, for many days); the demoniac crossing the apostles' path. The expositors observe the nature of the possession: "a spirit of divination" — a spirit of soothsaying or fortune-telling (a "Python" spirit, the Greek term); a real demonic possession, by which the girl uttered oracles and told fortunes; the reality of the demonic, the girl under the power of an evil spirit. The witnesses press the exploitation for gain: "which brought her masters much gain by soothsaying" — the girl's masters used her possession for profit, her soothsaying bringing them much gain; the demonic exploited for money; the girl a slave, doubly enslaved — to her masters and to the spirit; the greed that traded on her bondage. The expositors draw the lesson on the reality of demonic possession: the spirit of divination was a real demon, possessing the girl; not mere trickery (though her masters profited by it), but a real possession; the New Testament recognizing the reality of the demonic; the powers of darkness at work. The witnesses observe the contrast with the gospel: the slave girl, possessed and exploited, met the apostles, bringers of the gospel of liberty; the powers of darkness confronting the light; the gospel that would free the girl from the spirit (v.18) confronting the demonic that bound her. The expositors note the greed of the masters: the girl's bondage was their profit; her possession their gain; the love of money trading on the demonic and the slave's misery; the greed that would soon turn to rage when the gain was gone (v.19). The witnesses draw the lesson on the demonic and the gospel: the believer marks the slave girl possessed, the reality of the demonic, and the gospel that confronts the powers of darkness. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the spirit of divination, the exploitation for gain, and the gospel that frees the bound. The verse thus records the slave girl with a spirit of divination — possessed and exploited for gain — teaching the reality of the demonic possession and the greed that traded on her bondage.
17:
"The same followed Paul and us, and cried, saying, These men are the servants of the most high God, which shew unto us the way of salvation." The witnesses note the spirit's testimony to the apostles. Barnes conjectures why she did this — perhaps for gain (to be rewarded), or because she feared the apostles would expel the spirit and sought to placate them; the cause uncertain. The witnesses note the spirit, through the girl, proclaiming the apostles servants of the most high God who showed the way of salvation — a true testimony, yet from an evil source; the demon bearing witness to the truth, which Paul would not accept (v.18). The witnesses note the spirit's testimony. The witnesses note the true word from an evil source.

The witnesses note the spirit proclaiming the apostles servants of God — a true testimony from an evil source.

So the witnesses make the verse the spirit's testimony to the apostles. The lesson the expositors draw is the true word from an evil source, which Paul would not accept. The witnesses note the testimony: "These men are the servants of the most high God, which shew unto us the way of salvation" — the spirit, through the girl, proclaimed the apostles to be servants of the most high God, showing the way of salvation; a true statement — for so they were — yet uttered by the evil spirit. The expositors weigh why the spirit did this, which Barnes conjectures: perhaps for gain, that the girl might be rewarded for praising the apostles; or because she (the spirit) feared the apostles would expel her, and sought to placate them; or to gain credit by associating with them, mingling the demonic with the divine; the cause uncertain, but the testimony, though true, from an evil source. The witnesses press the truth of the words: the testimony was true — the apostles were servants of God, showing the way of salvation; the demon spoke the truth; yet a true word from an evil source. The expositors draw the lesson on Paul's refusal of the testimony (v.18): Paul would not accept the witness of the demon, though it was true; for the testimony of the evil spirit, even when true, was not fit to commend the gospel; the truth from an unclean source to be rejected, not welcomed; Christ likewise refused the testimony of demons (Mark 1:34, Luke 4:41); the gospel needs not, and accepts not, the witness of the devil. The witnesses observe the danger of the testimony: the spirit's proclaiming the apostles might have seemed to ally the gospel with the demonic, the soothsaying with the salvation; the mingling that would discredit the gospel; Paul's refusal guarding the gospel from such alliance. The expositors note the subtlety of Satan: the evil spirit, by a true testimony, sought to insinuate itself, to mingle with the gospel, to gain credit; the craft of Satan even in speaking the truth; the apostle discerning and refusing it. The witnesses draw the lesson on the true word from an evil source: the believer marks the spirit's true testimony, Paul's refusal of it, and the rejection of the truth from an unclean source. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the demon's testimony, the true word from an evil source, and the refusal to accept the witness of the devil. The verse thus records the spirit's testimony to the apostles — a true word from an evil source — teaching the rejection of the truth when it comes from the demonic, which Paul would not accept.
18:
"And this did she many days. But Paul, being grieved, turned and said to the spirit, I command thee in the name of Jesus Christ to come out of her. And he came out the same hour." The witnesses note the casting out of the spirit. Barnes explains Paul was "grieved" — molested, troubled, offended; because her testimony was troublesome, might seem to ally him with her, and was from an evil source. Gill notes the spirit was suffered to continue many days, that the expulsion might be the more manifest. The witnesses note Paul, grieved, commanding the spirit in the name of Jesus Christ to come out, and its immediate departure — the power of Christ's name over the demonic, the girl freed. The witnesses note the casting out. The witnesses note the power of the name.

The witnesses note Paul casting out the spirit in the name of Jesus — the power of Christ's name freeing the girl.

So the witnesses make the verse the casting out of the spirit. The lesson the expositors draw is the power of Christ's name over the demonic and the deliverance of the girl. The witnesses note Paul's grief: "Paul, being grieved" — Barnes explains he was molested, troubled, offended; grieved because the testimony was troublesome, might seem to ally him with the girl, and came from an evil source; the apostle disturbed by the demon's proclaiming him; his patience at last moved. The expositors observe the duration that Gill notes: "this did she many days" — the spirit was suffered to continue many days, that the expulsion might be the more manifest, witnessed by many; the providence delaying the deliverance for the greater testimony. The witnesses press the command: "I command thee in the name of Jesus Christ to come out of her" — Paul commanded the spirit, not in his own power, but in the name of Jesus Christ; the authority of Christ's name over the demonic; the apostle the instrument, Christ the power. The expositors draw the lesson on the power of the name: the spirit came out at the command in Christ's name; the name of Jesus mighty over the powers of darkness; the demonic subject to Christ; "in my name shall they cast out devils" (Mark 16:17); the power not the apostle's but the Lord's. The witnesses observe the immediate departure: "he came out the same hour" — the spirit departed at once, immediately; the instant deliverance, the power of the name effectual; the girl freed from the demon that bound her. The expositors note the deliverance of the girl: the slave girl, doubly enslaved, was freed from the spirit; the gospel of liberty setting her free from the demonic; the power of Christ liberating the bound; the compassion that freed her (though it cost the apostles, v.19). The witnesses draw the lesson on the power of Christ over the demonic: the believer marks the casting out in the name of Jesus, the power of the name, and the deliverance of the girl. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's command in Christ's name, the immediate departure of the spirit, and the power of Christ over the powers of darkness. The verse thus records the casting out of the spirit — in the name of Jesus, the spirit departing at once — teaching the power of Christ's name over the demonic and the deliverance of the bound girl.
19:
"And when her masters saw that the hope of their gains was gone, they caught Paul and Silas, and drew them into the marketplace unto the rulers," The witnesses note the masters' rage at their lost gain. Barnes presses the lesson: "the hope of their gains was gone... and this is as likely to enrage men as anything"; instead of regarding the act as a proof of divine power, they were intent only on their profits; "the love of it will blind them to all the truths of religion, and all the proofs of the power and presence of God." The witnesses note the masters, seeing their profit gone with the spirit, seizing Paul and Silas and dragging them to the rulers — the love of money turned to rage and persecution. The witnesses note the lost gain. The witnesses note the rage of greed.

The witnesses note the masters enraged at their lost gain — the love of money turned to persecution.

So the witnesses make the verse the masters' rage at their lost gain. The lesson the expositors draw is the blinding power of the love of money, turned to rage and persecution when the gain is lost. The witnesses note the cause of the rage: "when her masters saw that the hope of their gains was gone" — the masters, seeing that their profit was gone with the departed spirit, were enraged; Barnes notes the loss of gain is as likely to enrage men as anything; the love of money the root of their fury. The expositors observe the blindness of greed that Barnes marks: instead of regarding the casting out of the spirit as a proof of divine power, the masters were intent only on their profits; the love of money blinding them to the truths of religion and the proofs of God's power; the gain lost, not the miracle, their concern; the greed that sees only its own loss. The witnesses press the rage turned to persecution: "they caught Paul and Silas, and drew them into the marketplace unto the rulers" — the masters seized Paul and Silas (the leaders) and dragged them to the rulers; the love of money turned to violence and persecution; the greed that, robbed of its gain, attacks the apostles. The expositors draw the lesson on the love of money: it blinds men to the truth, hardens them against the proofs of God, and turns to rage when crossed; "the love of money is the root of all evil" (1 Timothy 6:10); the masters' greed the root of their persecution; the gain lost, the gospel hated. The witnesses observe the irony of the persecution: the apostles, who had done good — freeing the girl from the demon — are persecuted for it; the good deed repaid with violence; the gospel's mercy provoking the greedy; the benefactors of the girl attacked by her masters. The expositors note the contrast of motives: the apostles moved by compassion (freeing the girl), the masters by greed (their lost gain); the gospel's love against the world's avarice; the conflict of the kingdom of God and the love of money. The witnesses draw the lesson on the love of money and persecution: the believer marks the masters' rage at their lost gain, the blinding love of money, and the persecution it stirred. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the lost gain, the rage of greed, and the love of money turned to persecution. The verse thus records the masters' rage at their lost gain — seizing the apostles and dragging them to the rulers — teaching the blinding love of money, turned to rage and persecution when the gain is lost.
20:
"And brought them to the magistrates, saying, These men, being Jews, do exceedingly trouble our city," The witnesses note the accusation before the magistrates. Barnes notes the magistrates were the praetors (military rulers), Philippi being a Roman colony where the army officers exercised civil functions; and that the charge appealed to anti-Jewish prejudice. Gill notes the magistrates were the chief men of the city. The witnesses note the masters, hiding their real motive (the lost gain), accusing the apostles before the magistrates of being troublesome Jews disturbing the city — the false, prejudiced charge masking the true grievance. The witnesses note the accusation. The witnesses note the prejudiced charge.

The witnesses note the masters' accusation — masking their lost gain with a prejudiced charge against the "troublesome Jews."

So the witnesses make the verse the accusation before the magistrates. The lesson the expositors draw is the craft of the accusers, masking their real motive with a prejudiced, false charge. The witnesses note the tribunal: "brought them to the magistrates" — the masters brought Paul and Silas to the magistrates; Barnes notes these were the praetors (military rulers), Philippi being a Roman colony where the army officers exercised civil functions; the apostles arraigned before the city's authorities. The expositors observe the craft of the accusation: "These men, being Jews, do exceedingly trouble our city" — the masters did not state their real grievance (the loss of their gain), which would have exposed their greed, but accused the apostles of being troublesome Jews disturbing the city; the false charge masking the true motive; the craft of hiding avarice under a public-spirited complaint. The witnesses press the appeal to prejudice: "being Jews" — the accusation appealed to the anti-Jewish prejudice of the Romans (the Jews being often disliked and suspected); the masters stirring up prejudice against the apostles as Jews; the charge calculated to inflame the people and the magistrates against them; the appeal to bigotry. The expositors draw the lesson on the dishonesty of the accusers: they concealed their real motive (greed) and brought a false, prejudiced charge (troublesome Jews); the persecution of the gospel often masked under other pretexts; the true grievance hidden, a plausible charge advanced; the dishonesty of the persecutors. The witnesses observe the falseness of the charge: the apostles had not troubled the city, but done good (freeing the girl); the charge of disturbing the city false, the real disturbance the masters' own; the innocent accused of the accusers' crime; the false witness against the gospel. The expositors note the pattern of such accusations: the enemies of the gospel often mask their real motives (greed, envy, hatred) under public or religious pretexts; the persecution of the apostles charged as a public disturbance; the true cause hidden, the false advanced; the craft of the world against the gospel. The witnesses draw the lesson on the false charge: the believer marks the masters' accusation, the masking of their greed, and the prejudiced charge against the apostles. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the false accusation, the appeal to prejudice, and the dishonesty that masks greed with a public charge. The verse thus records the accusation before the magistrates — the masters masking their lost gain with a prejudiced charge — teaching the craft of the accusers and the false charge masking the true grievance.
21:
"And teach customs, which are not lawful for us to receive, neither to observe, being Romans." The witnesses note the second part of the charge — introducing unlawful customs. Barnes explains "customs" (ethe) refers to religious rites or modes of worship; the charge being that the apostles introduced a new religion unauthorized by Roman law — "a cunning and artful accusation." Gill notes the reference to the doctrine of salvation by Christ, taken as a new religious custom. The witnesses note the masters charging the apostles with teaching customs unlawful for Romans to receive — the appeal to Roman law and pride, masking the real grievance under a charge of introducing an illegal religion. The witnesses note the charge of unlawful customs. The witnesses note the appeal to Roman law.

The witnesses note the charge — teaching customs unlawful for Romans — masking the real grievance under an appeal to Roman law.

So the witnesses make the verse the second part of the charge. The lesson the expositors draw is the craft of the accusation, appealing to Roman law and pride to mask the real grievance. The witnesses note the charge: "teach customs, which are not lawful for us to receive, neither to observe, being Romans" — the masters charged the apostles with teaching customs (Barnes notes, religious rites or modes of worship) unlawful for Romans to receive or observe; the charge of introducing a new, unauthorized religion. The expositors observe the appeal to Roman law: the Romans permitted only authorized religions; to introduce a new, unsanctioned worship was unlawful; the masters charged the apostles with this — introducing an illegal religion among Romans; the appeal to the law against unauthorized cults. The witnesses press Barnes's note on the cunning of the charge: it was "a cunning and artful accusation" — appealing to Roman law and Roman pride ("being Romans"), masking the real grievance (the lost gain) under a charge of introducing an illegal religion; the craft of the accusers, advancing a plausible legal charge. The expositors draw the lesson on the masking of the real motive: the masters' true grievance was their lost gain (v.19), but they advanced a charge of illegal religion and public disturbance (v.20-21); the real motive hidden, the plausible charge advanced; the dishonesty of the persecutors, cloaking greed under legality. The witnesses observe the appeal to Roman pride: "being Romans" — the masters appealed to the pride of the Roman colonists, that they should not receive Jewish customs; stirring up national and religious prejudice; the appeal to bigotry and pride against the apostles. The expositors note the truth hidden in the charge: there was a sense in which the gospel did introduce a new religion, the worship of Christ; yet not as the masters meant, a public disturbance or an illegal cult, but the truth of God; the charge twisting the gospel into an illegal custom; the truth distorted into an accusation. The witnesses draw the lesson on the false legal charge: the believer marks the charge of unlawful customs, the appeal to Roman law and pride, and the masking of the real grievance. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the artful accusation, the appeal to prejudice, and the gospel charged as an illegal religion. The verse thus records the charge of teaching unlawful customs — appealing to Roman law and pride — teaching the craft of the accusation that masked the real grievance under a charge of illegal religion.
22:
"And the multitude rose up together against them: and the magistrates rent off their clothes, and commanded to beat them." The witnesses note the mob and the unjust beating. Barnes notes this was done "in a popular tumult, and without even the form of law" — a violation of the privileges of Roman citizens (of which Paul later complained, v.37), contrary to the laws; men affecting zeal for the law while violating it. Gill notes the multitude was stirred up by the masters and the rulers. The witnesses note the mob rising against the apostles, and the magistrates, without trial, tearing off their clothes and ordering them beaten — the injustice of the persecution, the form of law disregarded. The witnesses note the mob. The witnesses note the unjust beating.

The witnesses note the mob rising and the magistrates beating the apostles without trial — the injustice of the persecution.

So the witnesses make the verse the mob and the unjust beating. The lesson the expositors draw is the injustice of the persecution, the form of law disregarded in the popular tumult. The witnesses note the mob: "the multitude rose up together against them" — the crowd, stirred up by the masters and the rulers (Gill notes), rose against the apostles; the popular tumult, the mob inflamed by the charge; the multitude turned against the apostles. The expositors observe the disregard of law that Barnes marks: the beating was done in a popular tumult, without even the form of law — no trial, no defense, no proof; the magistrates yielding to the mob; a violation of the privileges of Roman citizens (of which Paul later complained, v.37), contrary to the laws; men affecting zeal for the law while violating it. The witnesses press the unjust beating: "the magistrates rent off their clothes, and commanded to beat them" — the magistrates, without trial, tore off the apostles' garments and ordered them beaten; the cruelty and the injustice; the apostles scourged uncondemned. The expositors draw the lesson on the injustice of the persecution: the apostles, innocent, were beaten without trial, condemned without hearing; the form of law disregarded in the tumult; the injustice of the persecution, the law itself violated by those who administered it. The witnesses observe the irony Barnes notes: men affect great zeal for the honour of the law, yet violate it; the magistrates, charged to uphold justice, yielding to the mob and beating the innocent uncondemned; the law professed and broken. The expositors note the suffering of the apostles: beaten with many stripes (v.23), uncondemned, by the unjust violence of the mob and the magistrates; the cost of the gospel, the apostles suffering injustice for Christ; "thrice was I beaten with rods" (2 Corinthians 11:25). The witnesses draw the lesson on the injustice the gospel meets: the believer marks the mob, the unjust beating without trial, and the injustice of the persecution. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the popular tumult, the form of law disregarded, and the apostles beaten uncondemned. The verse thus records the mob and the unjust beating — the magistrates beating the apostles without trial — teaching the injustice of the persecution, the form of law disregarded in the tumult.
23:
"And when they had laid many stripes upon them, they cast them into prison, charging the jailor to keep them safely:" The witnesses note the many stripes and the imprisonment. Barnes notes that, unlike the Jews (limited to forty stripes, 2 Corinthians 11:24), the Romans were unrestricted in the number; Paul's "in stripes above measure" (2 Corinthians 11:23) may refer to this; and they cast them into prison as a punishment. The witnesses note the apostles, after many stripes, cast into prison, the jailer charged to keep them safely — the severity of the suffering, the apostles imprisoned for the gospel after a cruel scourging. The witnesses note the many stripes. The witnesses note the imprisonment.

The witnesses note the apostles, after many stripes, cast into prison — the severity of their suffering for the gospel.

So the witnesses make the verse the many stripes and the imprisonment. The lesson the expositors draw is the severity of the apostles' suffering, scourged and imprisoned for the gospel. The witnesses note the severity of the scourging: "when they had laid many stripes upon them" — the apostles were beaten with many stripes; Barnes notes that, unlike the Jews (who were limited to forty, usually thirty-nine, 2 Corinthians 11:24), the Romans were unrestricted in the number of lashes, and probably inflicted many; Paul's phrase "in stripes above measure" (2 Corinthians 11:23) may refer to this cruel scourging; the severity of the beating. The expositors observe the imprisonment: "they cast them into prison, charging the jailor to keep them safely" — after the scourging, the apostles were cast into prison, the jailer charged to keep them securely; the punishment compounded, the suffering continued; the apostles imprisoned after the cruel beating. The witnesses press the suffering of the apostles for the gospel: scourged with many stripes, cast into prison, charged to be kept safely; the cost of the gospel, the apostles enduring cruel suffering for Christ; the servants treated as their Master; the fellowship of his sufferings (Philippians 3:10). The expositors draw the lesson on the cost of faithful witness: the apostles, for preaching the gospel and freeing the girl, were beaten and imprisoned; the world's enmity repaying their good with suffering; the price of faithfulness; "all that will live godly in Christ Jesus shall suffer persecution" (2 Timothy 3:12). The witnesses observe the providence in the imprisonment: the apostles cast into prison, charged to be kept safely — yet this very imprisonment would be the occasion of the jailer's conversion (v.27-34); the providence overruling the suffering for the gospel's advance; the prison the place of a conversion. The expositors note the patience of the apostles: scourged and imprisoned, they did not despair or murmur, but at midnight prayed and sang (v.25); the patience and joy of the suffering servants; the suffering borne with faith and praise. The witnesses draw the lesson on suffering for the gospel: the believer marks the many stripes, the imprisonment, and the severity of the apostles' suffering. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the cruel scourging, the imprisonment for the gospel, and the cost of faithful witness. The verse thus records the many stripes and the imprisonment — the apostles cast into prison after a cruel beating — teaching the severity of the apostles' suffering for the gospel.
24:
"Who, having received such a charge, thrust them into the inner prison, and made their feet fast in the stocks." The witnesses note the harsh securing of the apostles. Barnes notes the jailer thrust them into the inner prison — the most retired and secure part, more protected and harder to escape; and made their feet fast in the stocks (Greek, "made their feet secure to wood") — the painful confinement. Gill notes the jailer was doubly careful, having received so strict a charge. The witnesses note the jailer, charged to keep them safely, thrusting the apostles into the inner prison and fastening their feet in the stocks — the harsh, painful confinement; yet the setting of the midnight praise and the earthquake. The witnesses note the inner prison. The witnesses note the stocks.

The witnesses note the apostles thrust into the inner prison, their feet in the stocks — the harsh, painful confinement.

So the witnesses make the verse the harsh securing of the apostles. The lesson the expositors draw is the painful, secure confinement of the apostles, the setting for the midnight praise and the earthquake. The witnesses note the inner prison: "thrust them into the inner prison" — the jailer (Gill notes, doubly careful, having received so strict a charge) put the apostles into the inner prison; Barnes notes this was the most retired and secure part, more protected and harder to escape; the apostles placed in the most secure dungeon. The expositors observe the stocks: "made their feet fast in the stocks" — the jailer fastened the apostles' feet in the stocks (Barnes notes the Greek, "made their feet secure to wood"); the painful confinement, the feet stretched and held fast in the wooden instrument; the body racked, the prisoners secured and pained. The witnesses press the harshness of the confinement: the inner prison (dark, secure) and the stocks (painful, confining); the apostles, already scourged, now thrust into the worst part of the prison and racked in the stocks; the suffering compounded, the confinement harsh and painful. The expositors draw the lesson on the apostles' suffering: scourged, imprisoned, thrust into the inner dungeon, racked in the stocks; the depth of their suffering for the gospel; the servants enduring the worst; the cost of faithfulness. The witnesses observe the contrast with what follows: in this harsh confinement — the inner prison, the stocks, the pain — the apostles at midnight prayed and sang praises to God (v.25); the joy and praise rising from the dungeon; the suffering not quenching but heightening their faith; the praise from the place of pain.
