The expositors note the setting for the great events: the inner prison, the stocks, the midnight — the setting for the earthquake, the loosing of the bands, the jailer's conversion (v.26-34); the harsh confinement the stage of God's deliverance and a great conversion; the providence in the place. The witnesses draw the lesson on suffering and praise: the believer marks the inner prison, the stocks, and the harsh confinement, the setting of the midnight praise. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the painful confinement, the apostles racked in the stocks, and the setting for the deliverance to come. The verse thus records the harsh securing of the apostles — thrust into the inner prison, their feet in the stocks — teaching the painful confinement of the apostles, the setting for the midnight praise and the earthquake.
25:
"And at midnight Paul and Silas prayed, and sang praises unto God: and the prisoners heard them." The witnesses note the midnight praise of the suffering apostles. Chrysostom marvels: "What could equal these souls? These men had been scourged, had received many stripes, were in peril of their lives, were thrust into the inner prison, and set fast in the stocks: and for all this they did not suffer themselves to sleep, but kept vigil all the night. Do you mark what a blessing tribulation is?" The witnesses note Paul and Silas, despite their wounds and the harsh confinement, at midnight praying and singing praises to God, the prisoners hearing them — the joy and faith that turned the dungeon into a place of praise. The witnesses note the midnight praise. The witnesses note joy in suffering.

The witnesses note Paul and Silas praying and singing at midnight — joy and faith turning the dungeon into a place of praise.

So the witnesses make the verse the midnight praise of the apostles. The lesson the expositors draw is the joy and faith that turned the dungeon into a place of praise, an example of rejoicing in suffering. The witnesses note the praise: "at midnight Paul and Silas prayed, and sang praises unto God" — the apostles, at midnight, in the inner prison, their feet in the stocks, their backs torn with stripes, prayed and sang praises to God; the joy and faith rising from the dungeon; the suffering servants praising. The expositors observe Chrysostom's marvel: what could equal these souls? — scourged, wounded, in peril, thrust into the inner prison, set fast in the stocks; yet they did not sleep, but kept vigil, praying and singing; the blessing of tribulation, that drew from them not complaint but praise; the joy that suffering could not quench. The witnesses press the wonder of the praise in such circumstances: not murmuring, not despair, but prayer and praise; the apostles rejoicing in their suffering, singing in the night of their pain; the faith that praises in the dungeon; "rejoicing in tribulation" (Romans 12:12); the joy independent of circumstances. The expositors draw the lesson on rejoicing in suffering: the apostles, in the worst of suffering, prayed and praised; the gospel's joy triumphing over pain; the faith that turns the prison into a place of praise; the believer's joy not in circumstances but in God; the praise that rises from the place of pain. The witnesses observe the effect on the prisoners: "the prisoners heard them" — the other prisoners heard the praying and singing; the witness of the apostles' joy to their fellow-prisoners; the praise heard, the testimony borne; the suffering saints a witness even in the dungeon. The expositors note the contrast the praise displays: the world expects complaint and despair from the suffering; but the apostles prayed and praised; the difference of the gospel, the joy that the world cannot give nor take; the praise from the prison a testimony to the power of faith. The witnesses draw the lesson on joy and praise in suffering: the believer marks the midnight praise, the joy and faith of the apostles, and the dungeon turned to a place of praise. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the praying and singing at midnight, the rejoicing in suffering, and the witness of joy to the prisoners. The verse thus records the midnight praise of the apostles — praying and singing in the dungeon — teaching the joy and faith that turned the prison into a place of praise, an example of rejoicing in suffering.
26:
"And suddenly there was a great earthquake, so that the foundations of the prison were shaken: and immediately all the doors were opened, and every one's bands were loosed." The witnesses note the miraculous earthquake. Gill notes it was an extraordinary, supernatural earthquake — not from natural causes, but from the prayers and praises of the saints; God testifying his presence and power. Barnes notes the earthquake, in such circumstances, was a symbol of God's presence and an answer to prayer; designed to furnish proof of his presence and protection. The witnesses note the great earthquake shaking the prison, the doors opened and the prisoners' bands loosed — the miraculous answer to the apostles' prayer and praise, God's presence and power displayed. The witnesses note the earthquake. The witnesses note the doors opened.

The witnesses note the miraculous earthquake — the doors opened and bands loosed, God's answer to the apostles' praise.

So the witnesses make the verse the miraculous earthquake. The lesson the expositors draw is the miraculous answer to the apostles' prayer and praise, God's presence and power displayed. The witnesses note the suddenness and the supernatural character: "suddenly there was a great earthquake, so that the foundations of the prison were shaken" — Gill notes it was an extraordinary, supernatural earthquake, not from natural causes (pent-up wind) but from the prayers and praises of the saints; God testifying his presence and power; the earthquake an answer to the midnight praise. The expositors observe Barnes's note on the meaning: the earthquake, in such circumstances, was a symbol of God's presence and an answer to prayer (as Acts 4:31); designed to furnish proof of his presence and protection, and (the verses show) to lead to the jailer's conversion; the miracle a divine response. The witnesses press the effects: "immediately all the doors were opened, and every one's bands were loosed" — the prison doors flew open, and the bands of all the prisoners were loosed; the supernatural character shown in the effects — not merely the shaking, but the opening of the doors and the loosing of the bands; the miracle complete, the prisoners freed from their chains. The expositors draw the lesson on God's answer to prayer and praise: the apostles prayed and praised, and God answered with the earthquake; the prayer of the suffering saints prevailing; God's presence and power displayed in answer to their faith; "the effectual fervent prayer of a righteous man availeth much" (James 5:16). The witnesses observe the providence in the effects: the doors opened and the bands loosed — yet the prisoners did not flee (v.27-28); the providence opening the way, yet the apostles staying, that the jailer might be saved; the miracle ordered for the conversion to come. The expositors note the contrast with the apostles' calm: the earthquake terrified the jailer (v.27), but the apostles were calm, having prayed and praised; the difference between the servants of God in peace and the world in terror; the calm of faith amid the shaking. The witnesses draw the lesson on the miraculous answer: the believer marks the earthquake, the doors opened, and God's answer to the apostles' prayer and praise. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the supernatural earthquake, the loosing of the bands, and the power of God in answer to faith. The verse thus records the miraculous earthquake — the doors opened, the bands loosed — teaching the miraculous answer to the apostles' prayer and praise, God's presence and power displayed.
27:
"And the keeper of the prison awaking out of his sleep, and seeing the prison doors open, he drew out his sword, and would have killed himself, supposing that the prisoners had been fled." The witnesses note the jailer's despair. Barnes explains he supposed the prisoners had fled, and that their escape would be charged on him — it being customary to hold a jailer responsible for the safe keeping of prisoners, subject to the punishment due them (Acts 12:19); and that suicide was approved among the Greeks and Romans when encompassed with danger. Gill notes he was awakened by the earthquake. The witnesses note the jailer, awaking and seeing the doors open, drawing his sword to kill himself, supposing the prisoners gone — the despair of the heathen, ignorant of the true God. The witnesses note the jailer's despair. The witnesses note the attempted suicide.

The witnesses note the jailer about to kill himself in despair — supposing the prisoners fled, ignorant of the true God.

So the witnesses make the verse the jailer's despair. The lesson the expositors draw is the despair of the heathen, ignorant of the true God, contrasted with the hope the gospel would soon bring. The witnesses note the awakening: "the keeper of the prison awaking out of his sleep, and seeing the prison doors open" — the jailer, awakened by the earthquake (Gill notes), saw the prison doors open; alarmed, supposing the prisoners had fled. The expositors observe his despair: "he drew out his sword, and would have killed himself, supposing that the prisoners had been fled" — the jailer, thinking the prisoners gone, drew his sword to kill himself; Barnes explains he supposed their escape would be charged on him — it being customary to hold a jailer responsible for his prisoners, subject to the punishment due them (Acts 12:19); facing disgrace and death, he would take his own life. The witnesses press the heathen view of suicide that Barnes notes: among the Greeks and Romans, suicide was approved, even honoured, when a man was encompassed with danger or disgrace; the jailer, a heathen, ready to kill himself rather than face the shame; the despair of the heathen, without the hope of the gospel. The expositors draw the lesson on the despair of the unconverted: the jailer, in his alarm, saw no recourse but death; the despair of the heathen, ignorant of the true God, without hope; the darkness of the soul without the gospel; suicide the resort of despair. The witnesses observe the contrast with the gospel about to come: the jailer, in despair, ready to kill himself, would within the hour be brought to faith and joy (v.30-34); the despair of the night turned to the joy of salvation; the gospel bringing hope to the hopeless; the change from the sword of despair to the joy of faith. The expositors note Paul's intervention to come (v.28): the apostle would cry out, "Do thyself no harm; for we are all here"; the gospel staying the hand of despair; the servant of God preventing the suicide and pointing to salvation. The witnesses draw the lesson on despair and hope: the believer marks the jailer's despair, the heathen's hopelessness, and the gospel that would bring hope. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the attempted suicide, the despair of the unconverted, and the contrast with the joy the gospel would bring. The verse thus records the jailer's despair — about to kill himself, supposing the prisoners fled — teaching the despair of the heathen, ignorant of the true God, soon to be turned to joy by the gospel.
28:
"But Paul cried with a loud voice, saying, Do thyself no harm: for we are all here." The witnesses note Paul's compassionate cry staying the jailer's hand. Gill notes Paul knew, by divine revelation, what the jailer was about to do (though in the dark dungeon he could not see him), and perhaps knew God's gracious designs toward him and his family. Barnes draws the lesson: "Do thyself no harm — this is the solemn command of religion... it enjoins on men to do themselves no harm, by self-murder... in all cases Christianity seeks the true welfare of man." The witnesses note Paul, by divine knowledge, crying out to stay the jailer's self-murder, assuring him the prisoners were all there — the compassion of the gospel staying despair. The witnesses note Paul's cry. The witnesses note the staying of despair.

The witnesses note Paul's compassionate cry — "do thyself no harm" — staying the jailer's hand of despair.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's cry staying the jailer's hand. The lesson the expositors draw is the compassion of the gospel, staying the hand of despair and seeking the welfare of man. The witnesses note Paul's knowledge: "Paul cried with a loud voice" — Paul, in the inner dungeon, in the dark, could not see the jailer; yet (Gill notes) by divine revelation he knew what the jailer was about to do, and perhaps knew God's gracious designs toward him and his family; the apostle moved by divine knowledge and compassion to cry out. The expositors observe the cry: "Do thyself no harm: for we are all here" — Paul cried out to stay the jailer's self-murder, assuring him that the prisoners were all there, none fled; the compassion that prevented the suicide; the apostle, himself the prisoner, caring for the jailer's life. The witnesses press the compassion of the gospel that Barnes draws: "Do thyself no harm" is the command of religion in this and all cases; Christianity enjoins men to do themselves no harm — by self-murder, intemperance, lust, or dissipation; in all cases the gospel seeks the true welfare of man; the gospel the friend of life, the enemy of self-destruction. The expositors draw the lesson on the gospel and self-harm: the gospel says to every despairing soul, "Do thyself no harm"; it stays the hand of self-destruction, points to salvation, seeks the welfare of man; the gospel the answer to despair, the friend of the soul; "do thyself no harm" the word of life to the suicidal. The witnesses observe the remarkable compassion: Paul, scourged and imprisoned by this man's order, yet cares for his life; the love that returns good for evil; the apostle, the prisoner, saving the jailer; the compassion of the gospel even to the persecutor. The expositors note the providence in the cry: Paul's cry stayed the jailer's hand, that he might be saved (v.30-34); the providence preventing the suicide, that the despairing man might be brought to faith; the staying of death the prelude to the gift of life. The witnesses draw the lesson on the compassion of the gospel: the believer marks Paul's cry, the staying of despair, and the gospel that seeks the welfare of man. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the compassion that stayed the jailer's hand, the gospel's "do thyself no harm," and the love that cared for the persecutor's life. The verse thus records Paul's cry staying the jailer's hand — do thyself no harm, we are all here — teaching the compassion of the gospel, staying despair and seeking the welfare of man.
29:
"Then he called for a light, and sprang in, and came trembling, and fell down before Paul and Silas," The witnesses note the jailer's trembling approach. Gill draws the contrast: while under Satan's influence, going to a work of darkness (self-murder), he sought no light; but when delivered from the temptation, he calls for light; the change wrought. Barnes notes he came trembling, alarmed at the earthquake, amazed the prisoners remained, and confounded at the apostles' calmness. The witnesses note the jailer calling for a light, springing in, and falling trembling before Paul and Silas — the alarm and awakening of the man, brought from despair to reverence, on the verge of conversion. The witnesses note the trembling jailer. The witnesses note the awakened conscience.

The witnesses note the jailer springing in and falling trembling before the apostles — alarmed and awakened, on the verge of conversion.

So the witnesses make the verse the jailer's trembling approach. The lesson the expositors draw is the awakening of the jailer, brought from despair to reverence, on the verge of conversion. The witnesses note the calling for light: "he called for a light" — Gill draws the contrast: while under Satan's influence, going to the work of darkness (self-murder), the jailer sought no light; but now, delivered from the temptation, he calls for light; the change wrought, the man turning from darkness toward light; the symbol of his coming enlightenment. The expositors observe the trembling approach: "sprang in, and came trembling, and fell down before Paul and Silas" — the jailer sprang into the dungeon, trembling, and fell down before the apostles; Barnes notes he was alarmed at the earthquake, amazed that the prisoners remained, and confounded at the apostles' calmness; the alarm and awakening of the man. The witnesses press the trembling: the jailer came "trembling" — alarmed, awed, his conscience awakened; the terror of the earthquake, the wonder of the apostles' presence and calm, the sense of God's power, all working on him; the awakened conscience, the man trembling before the servants of God. The expositors draw the lesson on the awakening of the sinner: the jailer, brought from despair to reverence, trembling before the apostles, was on the verge of conversion; the awakening that precedes faith; the conscience stirred, the man humbled, the heart prepared; the trembling the beginning of the work. The witnesses observe the change in the jailer: from the despair that would kill itself (v.27) to the reverence that falls trembling before the apostles; the man turned from self-murder to seeking salvation; the change wrought by God's power, the heart prepared for the gospel. The expositors note the falling before the apostles: the jailer fell down before Paul and Silas — the jailer humbled before his prisoners; the reversal, the keeper at the feet of the kept; the man recognizing in the apostles the servants of God, humbling himself before them; the prelude to his question (v.30). The witnesses draw the lesson on the awakened conscience: the believer marks the jailer's trembling, the awakening from despair to reverence, and the heart prepared for conversion. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the jailer calling for light, falling trembling before the apostles, and the awakening that precedes faith. The verse thus records the jailer's trembling approach — calling for light, falling before the apostles — teaching the awakening of the jailer, brought from despair to reverence, on the verge of conversion.
30:
"And brought them out, and said, Sirs, what must I do to be saved?" The witnesses note the jailer's great question. Barnes marks its importance: "never was a more important question asked than this"; and shows the jailer meant his eternal salvation, not deliverance from danger — for the apostles' answer (believe on the Lord Jesus) referred to eternal salvation, having no effect on any temporal danger; the man asking how to be saved for ever. The witnesses note the jailer, awakened and trembling, bringing the apostles out and asking the great question — what he must do to be saved; the cry of the convicted soul seeking salvation. The witnesses note the great question. The witnesses note the convicted soul.

The witnesses note the jailer's great question — "what must I do to be saved?" — the cry of the convicted soul.

So the witnesses make the verse the jailer's great question. The lesson the expositors draw is the cry of the convicted soul seeking eternal salvation, the most important question a man can ask. The witnesses note the question: "Sirs, what must I do to be saved?" — the jailer, awakened and trembling, brought the apostles out of the dungeon and asked what he must do to be saved; Barnes marks that never was a more important question asked; the cry of the convicted soul seeking salvation. The expositors observe Barnes's showing that the jailer meant eternal salvation: he meant not deliverance from any temporal danger (the apostles' answer — believe on the Lord Jesus — would have no effect on any earthly peril), but eternal salvation; the man asking how to be saved for ever; the question of the soul's salvation. The witnesses press the importance of the question: "what must I do to be saved?" — the most important question a man can ask; the concern for the soul's salvation, the seeking of deliverance from sin and wrath; the awakened soul's cry; the question every sinner must ask. The expositors draw the lesson on the convicted soul: the jailer, awakened by God's power, convicted of his need, asks how to be saved; the conviction that precedes faith issuing in the great question; the soul, stirred and humbled, seeking salvation; the cry of the penitent. The witnesses observe the earnestness of the question: "what must I do?" — the jailer earnest, in earnest about his salvation; not idle curiosity, but the urgent concern of an awakened soul; the seriousness of one who feels his need; the earnestness that seeks an answer. The expositors note the providence in the question: the despairing jailer, stayed from suicide (v.28), awakened (v.29), now asks how to be saved; the providence bringing the man, through the night's events, to the great question; the despair and the awakening leading to the seeking of salvation; the question prepared by God's working. The witnesses draw the lesson on seeking salvation: the believer marks the jailer's great question, the cry of the convicted soul, and the seeking of eternal salvation. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the question "what must I do to be saved?", the earnestness of the awakened soul, and the concern for eternal salvation. The verse thus records the jailer's great question — what must I do to be saved? — teaching the cry of the convicted soul seeking eternal salvation, the most important question a man can ask.
31:
"And they said, Believe on the Lord Jesus Christ, and thou shalt be saved, and thy house." The witnesses note the great evangelical answer. Gill presses the nature of the faith required: "not with a bare historical faith... for there may be such a faith and no salvation; but so as to look unto him alone for life and salvation, to rely upon him, and trust in him; to commit himself, and the care of his immortal soul unto him." The witnesses note the answer to the great question: believe on the Lord Jesus Christ, and thou shalt be saved — salvation by faith in Christ; and "and thy house" — the same way of salvation held out to his household. The witnesses note salvation by faith. The witnesses note the household.

The witnesses note the great answer — believe on the Lord Jesus Christ, and thou shalt be saved — salvation by faith, the household embraced.

So the witnesses make the verse the great evangelical answer. The lesson the expositors draw is that salvation is by faith in Christ, held out to the jailer and his household. The witnesses note the answer: "Believe on the Lord Jesus Christ, and thou shalt be saved" — the apostles answer the great question simply and fully: believe on the Lord Jesus Christ, and thou shalt be saved; salvation by faith in Christ; the one way, the simple gospel. The expositors observe Gill's pressing of the nature of the faith: not a bare historical faith (a mere assent to facts, which may exist without salvation), but a saving faith — to look to Christ alone for life and salvation, to rely on him, trust in him, commit the soul to him, and expect pardon, righteousness, and life from him; the faith that saves, not mere notional belief. The witnesses press the simplicity and sufficiency of the answer: "believe... and thou shalt be saved" — nothing of works, nothing of the law, nothing to do but believe; the simple, sufficient way of salvation; faith in Christ the whole; the gospel's free offer. The expositors draw the lesson on salvation by faith: the jailer asked what he must do; the answer is, believe; salvation by faith in Christ, not by works; the way of salvation simple, free, sufficient; "by grace are ye saved through faith" (Ephesians 2:8); the great evangelical truth. The witnesses observe the embrace of the household: "and thy house" — the same way of salvation held out to the jailer's household; not that the jailer's faith would save them apart from their own, but that the same gospel was offered to them, and that they too, believing, would be saved (v.32, the word spoken to all; v.34, the household rejoicing in faith); the gospel embracing the family. The expositors weigh the meaning of "and thy house": some read it that the household would be saved by the same faith, each believing (the word being preached to all, v.32, and the household believing, v.34); the promise extending the offer to the family, not saving them by the jailer's faith alone; the household embraced in the gospel offer, each to believe. The witnesses draw the lesson on faith and the household: the believer marks the answer — believe and be saved — salvation by faith, the household embraced in the same offer. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the great answer, salvation by faith in Christ, and the gospel held out to the household. The verse thus records the great evangelical answer — believe on the Lord Jesus Christ, and thou shalt be saved — teaching salvation by faith in Christ, held out to the jailer and his household.
32:
"And they spake unto him the word of the Lord, and to all that were in his house." The witnesses note the preaching of the word to the jailer and his household. Gill expounds "the word of the Lord" as the gospel — the word of salvation, reconciliation, life, righteousness, and faith; that publishes salvation by Christ, and by which faith comes; spoken now to the jailer and all his house. The witnesses note the apostles, having directed the jailer to believe, now preaching the word of the Lord to him and to all in his house — the gospel fully proclaimed, that they might believe; faith coming by the word. The witnesses note the word preached. The witnesses note the household instructed.

The witnesses note the apostles preaching the word to the jailer and all his house — the gospel fully proclaimed, that they might believe.

So the witnesses make the verse the preaching of the word to the household. The lesson the expositors draw is that faith comes by the word, fully preached to the jailer and his house. The witnesses note the preaching: "they spake unto him the word of the Lord, and to all that were in his house" — the apostles, having directed the jailer to believe (v.31), now preached the word of the Lord to him and to all in his house; the gospel fully proclaimed, that they might understand and believe. The expositors observe Gill's exposition of "the word of the Lord": the gospel — the word of salvation, reconciliation, peace, life, righteousness, and faith; that publishes salvation by Christ, peace by his blood, life by his death, righteousness for justification, and proposes Christ as the object of faith; the word by which faith comes; spoken now to the jailer and his household. The witnesses press the necessity of the word for faith: the jailer was told to believe (v.31), but he must hear the word to believe; "faith cometh by hearing, and hearing by the word of God" (Romans 10:17); the apostles preaching the word, that faith might come; the word the means of faith. The expositors draw the lesson on the preaching that precedes faith: the command to believe is joined to the preaching of the word; the jailer and his house must hear the gospel before they can believe it; the word preached, the faith following; the means and the end joined. The witnesses observe the embrace of the household: "to all that were in his house" — the word was preached not to the jailer only, but to all his household; the gospel held out to the whole family; the household instructed, that they too might believe; the family embraced in the preaching, each to hear and believe. The expositors note the fullness of the instruction: though the hour was late (midnight) and the circumstances strange, the apostles fully preached the word; the gospel not given in a word only, but expounded; the household instructed in the way of salvation; the care to teach fully. The witnesses draw the lesson on the word and faith: the believer marks the preaching of the word to the household, the necessity of the word for faith, and the gospel proclaimed that they might believe. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the word of the Lord preached, the household instructed, and faith coming by the word. The verse thus records the preaching of the word to the household — to the jailer and all his house — teaching that faith comes by the word, fully preached, that they might believe.
33:
"And he took them the same hour of the night, and washed their stripes; and was baptized, he and all his, straightway." The witnesses note the jailer's tenderness and his baptism. Barnes marks the change: the apostles did not heal their own wounds (restoring others, not themselves); and "we have here a remarkable instance of the effect of religion in producing humanity and tenderness" — the same man who had thrust them into the inner prison and made their feet fast now washed their stripes. The witnesses note the jailer, that very hour, washing the apostles' wounds, and being baptized with all his household — the fruit of faith in tenderness and obedience; the household baptized. The witnesses note the washing of the stripes. The witnesses note the baptism.

The witnesses note the jailer washing the apostles' wounds and being baptized with all his house — the fruit of faith in tenderness and obedience.

So the witnesses make the verse the jailer's tenderness and baptism. The lesson the expositors draw is the fruit of faith in tenderness and obedience, the jailer washing the wounds and being baptized with his house. The witnesses note the tenderness: "he took them the same hour of the night, and washed their stripes" — the jailer, that very hour, washed the apostles' wounds — the stripes inflicted at the magistrates' order; Barnes marks the remarkable change — the same man who had thrust them into the inner prison and made their feet fast now tenderly washed their stripes; the effect of religion in producing humanity and tenderness; the converted jailer's love. The expositors observe that the apostles did not heal their own wounds: Barnes notes they had the gift of miracles, restoring others, but did not exercise it on themselves; their wounds washed by the jailer, not healed by their own power; the servants suffering, the convert ministering. The witnesses press the fruit of faith in tenderness: the jailer's first act after believing was to wash the apostles' wounds; the love that follows faith; the cruelty turned to tenderness; the converted heart showing itself in compassion; faith working by love (Galatians 5:6). The expositors draw the lesson on the change conversion works: the jailer, hours before harsh and cruel, now tender and kind; the transformation of the heart by grace; the convert's love to the servants of God; religion producing humanity and tenderness; the changed man. The witnesses note the baptism: "was baptized, he and all his, straightway" — the jailer was baptized at once, with all his household; the believer baptized upon faith, and his house also; the household baptism (as Lydia's, v.15) — debated between paedobaptists (the household, children included, baptized on the head's faith) and credobaptists (the household believers, baptized on their own faith; the word having been preached to all, v.32, and the household rejoicing in faith, v.34); the case cited both ways, the text not deciding expressly, but the household embraced in the ordinance. The witnesses draw the lesson on faith's fruit and baptism: the believer marks the washing of the wounds, the tenderness of the convert, and the household baptized. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the jailer's tenderness, the fruit of faith in love, and the baptism of the household. The verse thus records the jailer's tenderness and baptism — washing the wounds, baptized with all his house — teaching the fruit of faith in tenderness and obedience.
34:
"And when he had brought them into his house, he set meat before them, and rejoiced, believing in God with all his house." The witnesses note the jailer's hospitality and joy. Barnes notes "he set meat before them" (placed a table, food); and "rejoiced" — the effect of believing; religion produces joy; he was free from danger and alarm, had evidence of forgiveness and friendship with God; "what a change to be produced in one night!" The witnesses note the jailer bringing the apostles into his house, setting food before them, and rejoicing with all his house, believing in God — the joy of the new convert, the household believing and glad; the transformation complete. The witnesses note the jailer's hospitality. The witnesses note the joy of believing.

The witnesses note the jailer's hospitality and joy — rejoicing with all his house, believing in God; the transformation complete.

So the witnesses make the verse the jailer's hospitality and joy. The lesson the expositors draw is the joy that believing produces, the household rejoicing in faith, the transformation complete. The witnesses note the hospitality: "when he had brought them into his house, he set meat before them" — the jailer brought the apostles into his house and set food before them; Barnes notes he placed a table, food; the hospitality of the new convert, ministering to the servants of God; the love that follows faith, showing itself in kindness. The expositors observe the joy: "and rejoiced, believing in God with all his house" — the jailer rejoiced, with all his house, believing in God; Barnes notes the rejoicing was the effect of believing — religion produces joy; he was free from danger and alarm, had evidence of forgiveness and friendship with God; the joy of the new convert. The witnesses press the wonder of the change that Barnes marks: "what a change to be produced in one night!" — the jailer, hours before despairing and ready to kill himself (v.27), now rejoicing, believing, hospitable; the transformation complete; the despair turned to joy, the cruelty to tenderness, the darkness to light; the change wrought by grace in one night. The expositors draw the lesson on the joy of believing: faith produces joy; the new convert rejoices in the forgiveness of sins, the friendship of God, the hope of salvation; "joy unspeakable and full of glory" (1 Peter 1:8); the joy that follows faith; the believing heart a rejoicing heart. The witnesses observe the household believing: "believing in God with all his house" — not the jailer only, but all his house believed; the whole family brought to faith; the household saved (v.31), believing, rejoicing; the gospel embracing the family; the house believing with the head. The expositors note the completeness of the conversion: faith (believing in God), joy (rejoicing), love (hospitality), and the household embraced; the marks of a true and full conversion; the changed man and the changed house; the transformation entire. The witnesses draw the lesson on the joy of conversion: the believer marks the jailer's hospitality, the joy of believing, and the household believing and glad. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the joy of the new convert, the household believing, and the transformation wrought in a night. The verse thus records the jailer's hospitality and joy — rejoicing, believing with all his house — teaching the joy that believing produces and the transformation complete.
35:
"And when it was day, the magistrates sent the serjeants, saying, Let those men go." The witnesses note the magistrates' order to release the apostles. Matthew Henry infers the cause: the magistrates sent so early "as soon as it was day," intimating either that they were sensible the earthquake at midnight was meant to plead the cause of their prisoners, or that their consciences had smitten them for what they had done; while the persecuted sang in the stocks, the persecutors were full of tossings on their beds. The witnesses note the magistrates, the morning after the injustice, sending to release the apostles — perhaps moved by the earthquake or by conscience; the reversal beginning, the unjustly imprisoned to be freed. The witnesses note the order to release. The witnesses note the magistrates' change.

The witnesses note the magistrates ordering the apostles released — perhaps moved by the earthquake or by conscience.

So the witnesses make the verse the magistrates' order to release the apostles. The lesson the expositors draw is the change in the magistrates, perhaps moved by the earthquake or by conscience, beginning the reversal of the injustice. The witnesses note the order: "when it was day, the magistrates sent the serjeants, saying, Let those men go" — the morning after the unjust beating and imprisonment, the magistrates sent the officers to release the apostles; the order to free those they had unjustly imprisoned. The expositors observe Matthew Henry's inference about the cause: the magistrates sent so early, as soon as it was day, intimating either that they were sensible the earthquake at midnight was meant to plead the cause of their prisoners, or that their consciences had smitten them for what they had done; the early sending a sign of their unease. The witnesses press the contrast Henry draws: while the persecuted (Paul and Silas) sang in the stocks, the persecutors (the magistrates) were full of tossings on their beds; the suffering saints at peace, the unjust judges troubled; the conscience of the wrongdoers smitten, the night that brought joy to the apostles bringing unrest to their persecutors. The expositors draw the lesson on the working of conscience: the magistrates, having done injustice, were uneasy; the conscience smiting them; the unrest of those who do wrong; the persecutors troubled while the persecuted rejoiced; the difference between the peace of the righteous and the unrest of the unjust. The witnesses observe the beginning of the reversal: the magistrates, who had unjustly beaten and imprisoned the apostles, now send to release them; the reversal of the injustice beginning; the providence turning the tide; the unjustly imprisoned to be freed. The expositors note the providence in the change: God, perhaps by the earthquake and the working of conscience, moved the magistrates to release the apostles; the providence overruling the injustice, vindicating the apostles; the deliverance now coming. The witnesses draw the lesson on conscience and providence: the believer marks the magistrates' order, the working of conscience, and the providence beginning the reversal. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the early order to release, the unrest of the persecutors, and the providence turning the injustice. The verse thus records the magistrates' order to release the apostles — perhaps moved by the earthquake or conscience — teaching the change in the magistrates and the beginning of the reversal of the injustice.
36:
"And the keeper of the prison told this saying to Paul, The magistrates have sent to let you go: now therefore depart, and go in peace." The witnesses note the jailer's joyful report. Ellicott notes the jailer (now Paul's new convert) had probably learned to use "go in peace" in the fullness of its meaning, not as a mere conventional formula; he naturally looks on the offer — securing safety for his new friend — as one to be accepted. The witnesses note the jailer, with joy, reporting the magistrates' order to Paul, bidding him depart in peace — the new convert glad at his teacher's release, yet (the next verse) Paul having a further purpose. The witnesses note the jailer's report. The witnesses note "go in peace."

The witnesses note the jailer joyfully reporting the release — bidding Paul go in peace, the new convert glad at his teacher's freedom.

So the witnesses make the verse the jailer's joyful report. The lesson the expositors draw is the new convert's gladness at his teacher's release and the fuller meaning of "go in peace." The witnesses note the report: "the keeper of the prison told this saying to Paul, The magistrates have sent to let you go" — the jailer, now Paul's convert, reported the magistrates' order to release the apostles; the new convert bringing the news, with joy (Gill notes), of his teacher's freedom. The expositors observe Ellicott's note on "go in peace": the jailer, having spent a few hours with his new teacher, had probably learned to use "go in peace" in the fullness of its meaning — not as a mere conventional formula, but with the deeper sense of the peace the gospel gives; the new convert speaking from his new faith; the phrase full of meaning on his lips. The witnesses press the gladness of the convert: the jailer naturally looked on the offer — securing safety for his new friend — as one to be accepted; glad at his teacher's release, eager for his safety; the love of the convert for the apostle who had led him to Christ; the new bond of faith. The expositors draw the lesson on the convert's love: the jailer, lately the harsh keeper, now the loving convert, glad at his teacher's freedom; the love that follows faith, the bond between the convert and his teacher; the new affection of the believing heart. The witnesses observe the simplicity of the jailer's view: he supposed Paul would simply accept the release and depart; the natural assumption, not yet knowing Paul's further purpose (v.37); the convert's simple gladness, the apostle's larger design. The expositors note the contrast with what follows: the jailer bids Paul depart in peace, but Paul has a further purpose — to vindicate his Roman citizenship and the gospel against the injustice (v.37); the convert's simple report meeting the apostle's larger concern; the gladness and the further design. The witnesses draw the lesson on the convert's joy: the believer marks the jailer's joyful report, the fuller meaning of "go in peace," and the love of the convert for his teacher. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the new convert's gladness, the peace of the gospel, and the report of the release. The verse thus records the jailer's joyful report — go in peace — teaching the new convert's gladness at his teacher's release and the fuller meaning of the gospel's peace.
37:
"But Paul said unto them, They have beaten us openly uncondemned, being Romans, and have cast us into prison; and now do they thrust us out privily? nay verily; but let them come themselves and fetch us out." The witnesses note Paul's assertion of his Roman citizenship and his demand. Barnes lists the three aggravating circumstances Paul complains of: they had been beaten contrary to Roman law; publicly, so the reparation ought to be public; and uncondemned, without trial; therefore the magistrates ought themselves to come and release them. The witnesses note Paul, refusing to depart privily, asserting that he and Silas — Roman citizens — had been beaten openly, uncondemned, and imprisoned, and demanding that the magistrates come themselves to release them; the vindication of justice and the gospel. The witnesses note Paul's Roman citizenship. The witnesses note the demand for public vindication.

The witnesses note Paul asserting his Roman citizenship — demanding the magistrates come themselves, vindicating justice and the gospel.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's assertion of his citizenship and his demand. The lesson the expositors draw is the lawful use of civil rights to vindicate justice and the gospel against injustice. The witnesses note the complaint: "They have beaten us openly uncondemned, being Romans, and have cast us into prison" — Paul lists the aggravations; Barnes names three: they were beaten contrary to Roman law (which forbade scourging a Roman citizen), publicly (so the reparation ought to be public), and uncondemned (without trial); the threefold injustice against Roman citizens. The expositors observe the significance of Roman citizenship: to scourge a Roman citizen uncondemned was a grave violation of Roman law, severely punished; Paul and Silas, being Romans, had been treated unlawfully; the magistrates had broken the law in beating and imprisoning them; the injustice against citizens. The witnesses press Paul's demand: "now do they thrust us out privily? nay verily; but let them come themselves and fetch us out" — Paul refuses to be sent away privately, secretly; he demands that the magistrates come themselves and publicly release them; the public vindication answering the public injustice. The expositors draw the lesson on the lawful use of civil rights: Paul, who had borne the beating patiently, now asserts his Roman citizenship and demands justice; the lawful use of civil rights to vindicate justice; not for revenge, but to clear the gospel and the apostles of the charge of crime, and to protect the young church from future injustice; the right use of citizenship. The witnesses observe the purpose of the demand: not personal pride or revenge, but the vindication of justice and the gospel — that the magistrates' public injustice be publicly acknowledged, the apostles cleared, and the church protected; the public release a witness that the apostles were innocent, the gospel no crime; the vindication of the truth. The expositors note the balance in Paul: he bore the injustice patiently (singing in the stocks), yet now lawfully asserts his rights; the patience that endures and the wisdom that vindicates; not contradictory, but the use of lawful means at the right time; the believer free to use his civil rights for the gospel's good. The witnesses draw the lesson on the use of civil rights: the believer marks Paul's assertion of his citizenship, the demand for public vindication, and the lawful use of rights for justice and the gospel. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the threefold injustice, the demand that the magistrates come, and the vindication of justice and the gospel. The verse thus records Paul's assertion of his citizenship and demand — let them come themselves — teaching the lawful use of civil rights to vindicate justice and the gospel against injustice.
38:
"And the serjeants told these words unto the magistrates: and they feared, when they heard that they were Romans." The witnesses note the magistrates' fear at learning the apostles were Romans. Barnes notes they feared punishment for having imprisoned Roman citizens in violation of the laws — to punish unjustly a Roman citizen being a grave offence, severely punished (he cites the punishment of death and confiscation under the Valerian law, and Claudius depriving the Rhodians of freedom for crucifying Romans). The witnesses note the officers reporting Paul's words, and the magistrates seized with fear on learning the apostles were Roman citizens — the unjust judges now afraid, the tables turned. The witnesses note the magistrates' fear. The witnesses note the violation of Roman law.

The witnesses note the magistrates afraid on learning the apostles were Romans — the unjust judges now in fear of the law.

So the witnesses make the verse the magistrates' fear. The lesson the expositors draw is the fear of the unjust judges, who had violated Roman law in beating Roman citizens. The witnesses note the report and the fear: "the serjeants told these words unto the magistrates: and they feared, when they heard that they were Romans" — the officers reported Paul's words, and the magistrates were seized with fear on learning that the apostles were Roman citizens. The expositors observe the ground of the fear that Barnes marks: they feared punishment for having imprisoned and scourged Roman citizens in violation of the laws; to punish unjustly a Roman citizen was a grave offence, deemed an affront to the majesty of the Roman people, and severely punished; Barnes cites the Valerian law (death and confiscation for its violation) and Claudius depriving the Rhodians of freedom for crucifying Roman citizens; the magistrates in danger of severe penalty. The witnesses press the reversal: the magistrates, who had unjustly beaten and imprisoned the apostles, now feared; the unjust judges in fear of the law they had broken; the tables turned, the persecutors now afraid; the apostles, lately the victims, now in the position of advantage. The expositors draw the lesson on the fear of the wrongdoer: the magistrates, having done injustice, now feared the consequences; the fear that follows wrongdoing; the unjust judges trembling at the law they had violated; the reversal of the unjust. The witnesses observe the providence in the fear: God turned the tables — the magistrates who had oppressed the apostles now feared them; the providence vindicating the apostles, putting their persecutors in fear; the unjustly imprisoned now in the position to demand justice; the providence overruling the injustice. The expositors note the protection this afforded the church: the magistrates' fear would lead them to entreat the apostles and to be cautious in future; the vindication protecting the young church at Philippi from further injustice; the fear of the magistrates a shield for the church. The witnesses draw the lesson on the fear of the unjust: the believer marks the magistrates' fear, the violation of the law, and the reversal of the persecutors. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the unjust judges afraid, the tables turned, and the providence vindicating the apostles. The verse thus records the magistrates' fear — afraid on learning the apostles were Romans — teaching the fear of the unjust judges who had violated Roman law and the reversal of the persecutors.
39:
"And they came and besought them, and brought them out, and desired them to depart out of the city." The witnesses note the magistrates' humiliating entreaty. Barnes marks it "a most humiliating act for Roman magistrates; but in this case it was unavoidable" — the apostles had them in their power, and could effect their disgrace; probably they besought them by declaring them innocent and pleading ignorance, and desired them to depart to avoid further tumult and to secure themselves. The witnesses note the magistrates coming in person, beseeching the apostles, bringing them out, and asking them to leave the city — the proud rulers humbled, entreating those they had unjustly beaten; the gospel vindicated. The witnesses note the magistrates' entreaty. The witnesses note the humbling of the rulers.

The witnesses note the magistrates humbly entreating the apostles — the proud rulers humbled, the gospel vindicated.

So the witnesses make the verse the magistrates' humiliating entreaty. The lesson the expositors draw is the humbling of the proud rulers, beseeching those they had unjustly beaten, and the vindication of the gospel. The witnesses note the entreaty: "they came and besought them, and brought them out, and desired them to depart out of the city" — the magistrates came in person, beseeching the apostles, bringing them out of the prison, and asking them to leave the city; the rulers humbly entreating those they had unjustly beaten and imprisoned. The expositors observe Barnes's marking of the humiliation: it was a most humiliating act for Roman magistrates; but unavoidable — the apostles had them in their power, and could effect their disgrace and ruin; the proud rulers forced to humble themselves; the magistrates entreating their victims. The witnesses press the reversal: the magistrates, who had ordered the apostles beaten and imprisoned, now come to beseech them; the proud humbled, the persecutors entreating; the tables fully turned; the unjustly imprisoned now entreated by their judges; the vindication complete. The expositors draw the lesson on the humbling of the proud: the magistrates, lately so high-handed, now humbly entreat the apostles; the proud brought low; "he that exalteth himself shall be abased" (Luke 14:11); the rulers who oppressed now humbled before the oppressed; the reversal of the unjust. The witnesses observe the magistrates' motive that Barnes notes: probably they besought them by declaring them innocent and pleading ignorance, and desired them to depart — to avoid further tumult and to secure themselves from prosecution; the rulers seeking to cover their fault and avoid further trouble; the entreaty mingled with self-interest. The expositors note the vindication of the gospel: the public entreaty of the magistrates vindicated the apostles and the gospel — clearing them of crime, acknowledging the injustice, protecting the young church; the gospel cleared, the apostles honoured, the church established; the vindication serving the gospel. The witnesses draw the lesson on the vindication of the gospel: the believer marks the magistrates' entreaty, the humbling of the proud, and the vindication of the apostles and the gospel. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the rulers humbled, beseeching the apostles, and the gospel vindicated. The verse thus records the magistrates' humiliating entreaty — beseeching the apostles to depart — teaching the humbling of the proud rulers and the vindication of the gospel.
40:
"And they went out of the prison, and entered into the house of Lydia: and when they had seen the brethren, they comforted them, and departed." The witnesses note the apostles' visit to the brethren before departing. Barnes notes they "comforted them" — exhorted and encouraged them to persevere despite persecution; and "departed" — Paul and Silas; it appears Luke and Timothy remained at Philippi (Luke now speaking of Paul and Silas in the third person, the "we" ceasing until Acts 20:5). The witnesses note Paul and Silas, released, going to Lydia's house, comforting the brethren (the young church), and departing — the care for the new church, the encouragement amid persecution; the founding of the Philippian church complete. The witnesses note the visit to the brethren. The witnesses note the comforting of the church.

The witnesses note the apostles comforting the brethren at Lydia's before departing — the care for the young Philippian church.

So the witnesses make the verse the apostles' visit to the brethren. The lesson the expositors draw is the apostles' care for the young church, comforting and encouraging them before departing. The witnesses note the visit: "they went out of the prison, and entered into the house of Lydia: and when they had seen the brethren, they comforted them" — Paul and Silas, released, went to Lydia's house (the meeting place of the young church) and saw the brethren; Barnes notes they "comforted them" — exhorted and encouraged them to persevere despite the persecution; the care for the new converts. The expositors observe the apostles' priority: released from prison, their first care was the young church — to comfort and encourage the brethren; not to flee in fear, but to strengthen the believers; the pastoral care for the new church; the apostles' concern for the flock. The witnesses press the comforting amid persecution: the brethren, having seen the apostles beaten and imprisoned, needed comfort and encouragement; Paul and Silas, fresh from the prison, comforted them — encouraging them to persevere despite the opposition; the suffering apostles strengthening the young church; the encouragement to steadfastness. The expositors draw the lesson on the care for the young church: the apostles, before departing, established and comforted the new converts; the pastoral care that does not leave the new church untended; the encouragement to persevere; the founding of the church complete with the comforting of the brethren. The witnesses observe the departure and the "we" ceasing: "and departed" — Paul and Silas departed; Barnes notes it appears Luke and Timothy remained at Philippi (Luke now speaking of Paul and Silas in the third person, the "we" ceasing until Acts 20:5); some of the company staying to tend the young church; the wisdom of leaving labourers to establish the new converts. The expositors note the completeness of the Philippian mission: the church founded (Lydia, the jailer, the brethren), established, comforted; the Philippian church, dear to Paul (the Epistle to the Philippians), begun and tended; the firstfruits of Europe established. The witnesses draw the lesson on the care for the church: the believer marks the apostles comforting the brethren, the care for the young church, and the founding of the Philippian church. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the visit to the brethren, the comforting amid persecution, and the establishing of the new church. The verse thus records the apostles' visit to the brethren — comforting them before departing — teaching the apostles' care for the young Philippian church, comforting and encouraging them amid persecution.
